csharp_intro.title: C# Overview
csharp_intro.summary: A modern, type-safe language for the .NET ecosystem — apps, web, cloud and game dev.
csharp_intro.details:
C# is a statically typed, object-oriented language developed by Microsoft as part of the .NET platform. Modern C# evolves rapidly (records, pattern matching, nullable reference types, async streams). Learn the .NET runtime (CLR), toolchain (dotnet CLI), and common workloads: ASP.NET for web, libraries for services, Xamarin/MAUI for mobile, and Unity for games.

Key Concepts:
1. Type System: Strong static typing with value/reference types, generics, and interfaces
2. Memory Management: Automatic garbage collection with manual control via unsafe context
3. Paradigms: Object-oriented, functional (LINQ, lambda expressions), and concurrent programming
4. Modern Features: Local functions, tuple deconstruction, pattern matching, nullable references
5. Platform Support: Windows, Linux, macOS, iOS, Android via .NET Multi-platform App UI
6. Development Tools: Visual Studio, VS Code with C# Dev Kit, Rider, and command-line tools
7. Frameworks: .NET Framework (Windows), .NET Core (cross-platform), Mono (mobile/games)

interview.q: What is .NET?
interview.a: .NET is a cross-platform runtime and SDK for building apps; it includes CLR (Common Language Runtime), BCL (Base Class Library), and SDK tooling for development, building, and deployment.

interview.q: Why choose C#?
interview.a: C# offers excellent productivity, rich libraries, strong tooling (Visual Studio/VS Code), excellent performance, and broad platform support via .NET. It combines object-oriented and functional programming paradigms with modern language features.

interview.q: What is the difference between .NET Framework and .NET Core?
interview.a: .NET Framework is Windows-only and includes legacy technologies like Web Forms and WCF. .NET Core (now .NET 5+) is cross-platform, open-source, modular, and includes modern web frameworks like ASP.NET Core. .NET Framework 4.x is in maintenance mode.

interview.q: Explain value types vs reference types in C#
interview.a: Value types (struct, enum, primitive types) store data directly, allocated on stack, passed by value. Reference types (class, interface, delegate, array) store references to data, allocated on heap, passed by reference. Nullable value types bridge both worlds.

interview.q: What is the CLR (Common Language Runtime)?
interview.a: CLR is the execution engine of .NET that manages memory (garbage collection), provides JIT compilation from IL to native code, handles exceptions, enforces security, and manages threads. It enables language interoperability through Common Type System (CTS).

interview.q: What are async/await keywords and how do they work?
interview.a: async/await enables asynchronous programming without blocking threads. 'async' marks a method as asynchronous. 'await' pauses method execution until awaited task completes, then resumes on captured synchronization context. Under the hood, it uses state machines.

interview.q: Explain garbage collection in .NET
interview.a: .NET uses generational garbage collection with three generations (0, 1, 2). Gen 0 collects short-lived objects frequently. GC identifies reachable objects using mark-and-sweep, compacts memory, and manages large object heap separately. Finalizers run on separate thread.

interview.q: What are delegates and events in C#?
interview.a: Delegates are type-safe function pointers that define method signatures. Events are wrappers around delegates following publisher-subscriber pattern. Events use += and -= for subscription, ensuring only event owner can invoke it. Used extensively in GUI and observer patterns.

interview.q: What is LINQ and how does it work?
interview.a: Language Integrated Query (LINQ) provides query capabilities integrated into C# syntax. It works with IEnumerable<T> and IQueryable<T>. Two flavors: LINQ to Objects (in-memory) and LINQ to SQL/Entities (database queries). Uses extension methods and lambda expressions.

interview.q: Explain inheritance vs composition in C#
interview.a: Inheritance creates 'is-a' relationships (class B : A), enabling code reuse but creating tight coupling. Composition creates 'has-a' relationships (class B { private A a; }), promoting loose coupling and flexibility. Favor composition over inheritance for maintainability.

interview.q: What are extension methods?
interview.a: Extension methods allow adding methods to existing types without modifying them. Defined as static methods in static classes with 'this' keyword before first parameter. Called as instance methods. Used in LINQ and for adding functionality to sealed classes.

interview.q: What is dependency injection in .NET?
interview.a: DI is a design pattern where dependencies are provided to classes rather than created internally. .NET Core includes built-in DI container in Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection. Supports singleton, scoped, and transient lifetimes. Promotes loose coupling and testability.

interview.q: Explain nullable reference types (C# 8+)
interview.a: Feature to reduce null reference exceptions. When enabled, reference types are non-nullable by default. Use '?' to make nullable (string? name). Compiler performs static flow analysis to warn about potential null dereferences. Requires annotations and proper initialization.

interview.q: What are records in C# 9+?
interview.a: Records are reference types with value-based equality, primarily for immutable data. Provide built-in GetHashCode(), ToString(), and copy semantics. Support positional records (record Person(string Name, int Age)) and 'with' expressions for non-destructive mutation.

interview.q: What is pattern matching in C#?
interview.a: Feature to test values against patterns and extract information. Includes type patterns (obj is string s), property patterns (obj is Person { Age: > 18 }), tuple patterns, and relational patterns. Used in 'is' expressions, 'switch' statements, and 'switch' expressions.

interview.q: Explain the using statement and IDisposable
interview.a: IDisposable interface defines Dispose() method for resource cleanup. 'using' statement ensures Dispose() is called even if exception occurs (syntactic sugar for try-finally). 'using' declaration (C# 8+) disposes at scope exit. Used for files, database connections, etc.

interview.q: What are attributes in C#?
interview.a: Attributes add metadata to code elements (classes, methods, properties). Defined as classes inheriting from System.Attribute. Used for serialization ([Serializable]), validation ([Required]), API documentation ([Obsolete]), and custom annotations. Accessible via reflection.

interview.q: What is reflection in .NET?
interview.a: Reflection allows inspecting and manipulating types at runtime. Access via System.Type, Assembly, MethodInfo, PropertyInfo classes. Used for dependency injection, serialization, ORMs, and dynamic code generation. Has performance overhead compared to static code.

interview.q: Explain task parallel library (TPL)
interview.a: TPL provides APIs for parallel programming and asynchronous operations. Includes Task class for async operations, Parallel class for data parallelism (ForEach, For), and PLINQ for parallel queries. Manages thread pool, cancellation, and exception aggregation.

interview.q: What is Entity Framework Core?
interview.a: EF Core is an open-source, cross-platform ORM for .NET. Maps database tables to entity classes (Code First or Database First). Supports LINQ queries, change tracking, migrations, and multiple database providers (SQL Server, PostgreSQL, SQLite). Implements Unit of Work and Repository patterns.

interview.q: How does .NET handle versioning?
interview.a: .NET uses semantic versioning (Major.Minor.Patch). Side-by-side installation supported. Assembly versioning includes version, culture, and public key token. Strong-named assemblies for global assembly cache. .NET Core supports app-local deployment (self-contained or framework-dependent).

interview.q: What is Middleware in ASP.NET Core?
interview.a: Middleware components handle HTTP requests and responses in a pipeline. Each component can process request, call next middleware, and process response. Built-in middleware for routing, authentication, static files, CORS. Configured via Use, Run, Map methods in Startup.Configure.

csharp_syntax.title: Syntax & Types
csharp_syntax.summary: var, explicit types, nullable reference types, pattern matching, tuples and local functions.
csharp_syntax.details:
C# syntax is expressive: use 'var' for local inference, nullable reference types (C# 8+) to catch nulls, pattern matching for concise branching, records for immutable data, and tuples for lightweight grouping. Understand value vs reference types (struct vs class) and boxing/unboxing.

Type System Hierarchy:
1. Value Types: Primitive types (int, bool, char), structs, enums
2. Reference Types: Classes, interfaces, delegates, arrays, strings
3. Special Types: dynamic (runtime binding), object (base type), void (no return)

Key Syntax Features:
• Type Inference: var keyword for local variables with explicit initialization
• Nullable Types: Value types with '?' suffix (int?) and nullable reference types (string?)
• Pattern Matching: Type patterns, property patterns, switch expressions
• Tuples: Lightweight grouping with named or unnamed elements
• Records: Concise syntax for immutable reference types with value semantics
• Local Functions: Nested functions within methods for helper logic
• Ref Returns/Parameters: Pass or return by reference for performance
• Ranges/Indices: C# 8+ syntax for slicing collections

interview.q: What is the difference between struct and class?
interview.a: structs are value types allocated on stack (or inline in containing type), have default parameterless constructor, cannot inherit, and are copied by value. classes are reference types allocated on heap, support inheritance, have explicit constructors, and are copied by reference. Use structs for small, immutable data; classes for complex objects with identity semantics.

interview.q: What are nullable reference types?
interview.a: C# 8+ compiler feature that distinguishes between non-nullable (T) and nullable (T?) reference types. When enabled (#nullable enable), reference types are non-nullable by default. The compiler performs static flow analysis to warn about potential null dereferences, reducing NullReferenceExceptions. Requires proper initialization and null checking.

interview.q: When should you use 'var' vs explicit type declaration?
interview.a: Use 'var' when: 1) Type is obvious from right side (var list = new List<string>()), 2) Working with anonymous types, 3) Long generic type names. Use explicit types when: 1) Type isn't obvious, 2) Initial value is null, 3) Working with numeric types where precision matters. Favor readability and maintainability.

interview.q: Explain pattern matching in C# with examples
interview.a: Pattern matching tests values against patterns: 1) Type patterns (obj is string s), 2) Property patterns (obj is Person { Age: > 18 }), 3) Tuple patterns ((0, 0) => Origin), 4) Relational patterns (age is > 18 and < 65), 5) Logical patterns (and, or, not). Used in 'is' expressions, 'switch' statements, and 'switch' expressions for concise conditional logic.

interview.q: What are records and when should you use them?
interview.a: Records (C# 9+) are reference types with value-based equality, primarily for immutable data. They provide built-in GetHashCode(), ToString(), and copy semantics via 'with' expressions. Use records for DTOs, value objects, and immutable data carriers. They reduce boilerplate code for equality comparison and string representation.

interview.q: How do tuples differ from classes or anonymous types?
interview.a: Tuples are lightweight value types for temporary grouping, support named elements, can be returned from methods, and are mutable by default. Classes are for complex objects with behavior. Anonymous types are reference types created with 'new { }', are immutable, and have limited scope (usually within method). Tuples bridge the gap between these with better performance than classes.

interview.q: What is boxing and unboxing, and what are their performance implications?
interview.a: Boxing converts value type to object (reference type), allocating on heap. Unboxing converts object back to value type, with type checking. Both have performance overhead: boxing allocates memory, unboxing requires type check and copy. Avoid in performance-critical code; use generics (List<int> instead of ArrayList) to prevent boxing.

interview.q: Explain the 'ref', 'in', and 'out' parameter modifiers
interview.a: 'ref' passes parameter by reference, allowing modification. 'in' passes read-only reference for large structs, preventing copying. 'out' similar to ref but parameter must be assigned in method. 'ref readonly' returns read-only reference. Use for performance with large structs, or when method needs to modify arguments.

interview.q: What are local functions and their advantages?
interview.a: Local functions are nested methods within another method. Advantages: 1) Can capture variables from enclosing scope, 2) Can appear after return statements, 3) Can be iterators or async methods, 4) Don't pollute class namespace, 5) Can be private to method's implementation. Useful for helper methods used only within one method.

interview.q: What is the difference between 'readonly struct' and 'record struct'?
interview.a: 'readonly struct' is an immutable value type where all fields are readonly. 'record struct' (C# 10+) is a struct with synthesized equality members and 'with' expressions. Both promote immutability: readonly struct enforces it at compile time, record struct provides value semantics with mutation via 'with'.

interview.q: How does type inference work with 'var'?
interview.a: Compiler determines type from initialization expression: 1) For literals (var x = 5 → int), 2) For constructor calls (var list = new List<string>() → List<string>), 3) For method returns, 4) Cannot be used for fields, parameters, or return types. Compile-time only, doesn't affect runtime performance.

interview.q: What are nullable value types vs nullable reference types?
interview.a: Nullable value types (int?) existed before C# 8, wrapped value types in Nullable<T> struct. Nullable reference types (string?) are compile-time annotations with static analysis. Both use '?' syntax but work differently: value types use runtime wrapper, reference types are compiler warnings.

interview.q: Explain the 'is' operator vs 'as' operator
interview.a: 'is' checks type compatibility and returns bool, can be used with pattern matching. 'as' attempts conversion, returns null if fails (only for reference types). 'is' doesn't throw exceptions, 'as' is efficient for reference type conversions. Prefer 'is' with pattern matching for safety and expressiveness.

interview.q: What are extension methods and how do they work?
interview.a: Extension methods allow adding methods to existing types without modifying them. Defined as static methods in static classes with 'this' modifier on first parameter. Called as instance methods. Compiler translates 'obj.Extension()' to 'StaticClass.Extension(obj)'. Used extensively in LINQ.

interview.q: How do you handle null values in modern C#?
interview.a: 1) Nullable reference types for compile-time checking, 2) Null-coalescing operator (??) for defaults, 3) Null-conditional operator (?.) for safe member access, 4) Null-forgiving operator (!.) to suppress warnings, 5) ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull() for parameter validation, 6) Pattern matching (obj is null).

interview.q: What are default literal expressions?
interview.a: C# 7.1+ feature where 'default' can infer type from context. Instead of 'default(int)' or 'default(string?)', use 'default'. Useful in generic code, switch expressions, and when type is obvious: 'int x = default;', 'string? s = default;'. Improves readability.

interview.q: Explain the 'nameof' operator and its uses
interview.a: 'nameof' returns string name of variable, type, or member at compile-time. Uses: 1) Argument validation (nameof(parameter)), 2) INotifyPropertyChanged, 3) Logging and exceptions, 4) ASP.NET Core route names, 5) Dependency property names. Refactor-safe and avoids magic strings.

interview.q: What are ranges and indices in C# 8+?
interview.a: Indices (^) count from end: ^1 is last element. Ranges (..) specify subranges: array[1..4], array[..^1]. Works with arrays, Span<T>, strings. Compiler translates to calls to GetSubstring/GetSlice methods. Provides concise syntax for common slicing operations.

interview.q: How do you implement value-based equality in C#?
interview.a: 1) For records: built-in via 'record' keyword, 2) For classes: override Equals(), GetHashCode(), implement IEquatable<T>, 3) For structs: implement IEquatable<T> for performance, 4) Consider implementing == and != operators, 5) Use records for DTOs to avoid manual implementation.

interview.q: What is the 'required' modifier in C# 11?
interview.a: 'required' modifier on properties/fields forces callers to initialize them via object initializer or constructor. Ensures type is fully initialized. Works with constructors, init-only setters. Provides compile-time safety for mandatory properties, reducing runtime errors.

csharp_oop.title: OOP & .NET Types
csharp_oop.summary: Classes, interfaces, inheritance, explicit interface implementation, and extension methods.
csharp_oop.details:
Design classes with clear responsibilities. Use interfaces for contracts and prefer composition. Understand extension methods, partial classes, records, and access modifiers. Learn how the BCL models common abstractions and when to use sealed/abstract.

OOP Pillars in C#:
1. Encapsulation: Access modifiers (public, private, protected, internal), properties, indexers
2. Inheritance: Base class inheritance, virtual/override methods, abstract classes
3. Polymorphism: Method overriding, interface implementation, method overloading
4. Abstraction: Interfaces, abstract classes, dependency injection

Advanced Concepts:
• Explicit Interface Implementation: Implement interface members with explicit names
• Partial Classes/Methods: Split class definition across files, partial methods for extensibility
• Static Classes/Constructors: Classes with only static members, type initialization
• Nested Types: Classes defined within other classes for encapsulation
• Access Modifiers: Assembly-level (internal, protected internal) and type-level access
• Object Lifecycle: Constructors, finalizers, and initialization order
• Covariance/Contravariance: Type parameter variance in interfaces and delegates

interview.q: When to use interfaces vs abstract classes?
interview.a: Use interfaces for contracts when multiple implementations exist, or when multiple inheritance is needed. Use abstract classes when: 1) You need to share common implementation, 2) You need to control access with protected members, 3) You need constructors, 4) You want to force derived classes to provide specific behavior. Prefer interfaces for flexibility and testability.

interview.q: What are extension methods and their limitations?
interview.a: Extension methods allow adding methods to existing types without modifying them. Limitations: 1) Cannot access private/protected members, 2) Cannot override existing methods, 3) Lower priority than instance methods, 4) Only work on types, not individual objects, 5) Need to be in static class, 6) First parameter must be of extended type with 'this' modifier. Use for utility methods and enhancing sealed classes.

interview.q: Explain polymorphism in C# with examples
interview.a: Polymorphism allows objects to be treated as their base type while executing derived type behavior. Types: 1) Compile-time (overloading), 2) Runtime (overriding). Implement via: virtual/override methods, interface implementation, abstract methods. Example: Animal reference holding Dog object calls Dog's overridden methods. Enables extensible design and dependency inversion.

interview.q: What is the difference between 'virtual' and 'abstract' methods?
interview.a: Virtual methods have implementation in base class and can be overridden in derived classes (optional). Abstract methods have no implementation, must be overridden in concrete derived classes, and can only exist in abstract classes. Virtual methods provide default behavior; abstract methods define contracts that must be implemented.

interview.q: When should you use 'sealed' keyword?
interview.a: Use 'sealed' on classes to prevent inheritance when: 1) Class is designed as complete/final, 2) Security concerns (preventing tampering), 3) Performance optimizations (JIT can inline virtual calls), 4) Library design (controlling extensibility). Use on methods to prevent further overriding in derived classes. Sealed classes enable certain compiler optimizations.

interview.q: What are partial classes and when to use them?
interview.a: Partial classes allow splitting class definition across multiple files. Use when: 1) Auto-generated code (designer files), 2) Large classes that benefit from separation, 3) Team development on same class, 4) Separating interface implementation from core logic. Partial methods allow optional hooks in generated code.

interview.q: Explain explicit interface implementation and its use cases
interview.a: Explicit interface implementation hides interface members unless object is cast to interface. Use when: 1) Implementing multiple interfaces with same member names, 2) Hiding interface members from public API, 3) Providing different implementations for same method in different interfaces, 4) Versioning (adding interface to existing class without breaking API).

interview.q: What is the difference between 'protected internal' and 'private protected'?
interview.a: 'protected internal': accessible from derived classes OR classes in same assembly. 'private protected': accessible only from derived classes within same assembly. C# 7.2+ feature for finer access control. Use private protected for members that should only be accessible to derived classes within the same codebase.

interview.q: How do you implement the Singleton pattern in C#?
interview.a: Multiple approaches: 1) Static constructor (thread-safe), 2) Double-check locking, 3) Lazy<T> wrapper, 4) Using dependency injection container. Modern approach: Use Microsoft's dependency injection with AddSingleton(). For manual implementation, Lazy<T> provides thread-safe initialization with good performance.

interview.q: What are covariance and contravariance in C#?
interview.a: Variance enables implicit reference conversion for generic interfaces/delegates. Covariance (out): IEnumerator<Derived> → IEnumerator<Base> (preserves assignment compatibility). Contravariance (in): IComparer<Base> → IComparer<Derived> (reverses assignment). Supported in: IEnumerable<T> (covariant), IComparer<T> (contravariant). Enables more flexible generic programming.

interview.q: Explain the Factory Method and Abstract Factory patterns
interview.a: Factory Method: Define interface for creating object, let subclasses decide which class to instantiate. Use when class can't anticipate class of objects it must create. Abstract Factory: Provide interface for creating families of related objects without specifying concrete classes. Use when system needs to be independent of how objects are created, composed, represented.

interview.q: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)?
interview.a: LSP states: Derived types must be substitutable for their base types without affecting correctness. In C#: 1) Preconditions cannot be strengthened, 2) Postconditions cannot be weakened, 3) Invariants must be preserved, 4) Exception types must match. Violations: Throwing NotSupportedException, returning null when base returns non-null.

interview.q: How does C# handle multiple inheritance?
interview.a: C# doesn't support multiple class inheritance (one base class only). Supports multiple interface inheritance (class can implement multiple interfaces). Use composition (containing objects) to combine behaviors from multiple classes. Default interface methods (C# 8+) allow interface implementations, partially addressing multiple inheritance needs.

interview.q: What are static constructors and when are they called?
interview.a: Static constructors initialize static members, run once per type before any instance is created or any static member is accessed. Characteristics: 1) No parameters, 2) No access modifiers, 3) Cannot be called directly, 4) Thread-safe execution guaranteed by runtime. Use for one-time initialization of static fields or type registration.

interview.q: Explain the difference between 'new' and 'override' keywords
interview.a: 'override': Extends or modifies virtual/abstract method implementation, participates in polymorphism. 'new': Hides inherited method with same name, doesn't participate in polymorphism (method chosen based on compile-time type). Use 'new' when you intentionally want to hide base implementation; usually indicates design smell.

interview.q: What are indexers in C# and how to use them?
interview.a: Indexers allow objects to be indexed like arrays. Defined with 'this' keyword: public T this[int index] { get; set; }. Can be overloaded with different parameter types. Use for collection-like classes, matrices, or when natural indexing semantics exist. Can have multiple parameters for multi-dimensional indexing.

interview.q: How do you implement deep copy in C#?
interview.a: Methods: 1) Manual copying (copy constructor or Clone method), 2) Serialization/deserialization (BinaryFormatter, but deprecated), 3) MemberwiseClone for shallow copy, 4) Using AutoMapper or similar libraries, 5) For records: 'with' expressions create new copies. Consider ICloneable interface, though it has design issues (shallow vs deep ambiguity).

interview.q: What are nested classes and when to use them?
interview.a: Nested classes are defined within another class. Use when: 1) Class is only used by containing class, 2) Need access to private members of containing class, 3) Implementing builder/factory pattern, 4) Creating comparers or specialized iterators. Can be public, private, protected, etc. Outer class can create nested class instances.

interview.q: Explain the Strategy pattern implementation in C#
interview.a: Strategy pattern defines family of algorithms, encapsulates each, makes them interchangeable. Implement with: 1) Interface defining algorithm, 2) Concrete strategy classes implementing interface, 3) Context class that uses strategy interface. Use when you need multiple algorithms for same task, or to avoid conditional statements for algorithm selection.

interview.q: What are default interface methods (C# 8+) and their use?
interview.a: Default interface methods allow adding methods to interfaces with implementation, without breaking existing implementers. Use for: 1) Adding functionality to interfaces over time, 2) Creating mixin-like behavior, 3) Providing helper methods for interface, 4) Simulating traits. Implementers can override default implementations. Requires runtime support (.NET Core 3.0+).

csharp_memory.title: Memory, GC & IDisposable
csharp_memory.summary: Garbage-collected runtime, IDisposable pattern, using/async using and memory pooling.
csharp_memory.details:
C# runs on a GC-managed runtime; manage unmanaged resources with IDisposable and 'using' statements. Use ArrayPool<T> and Span<T>/Memory<T> for high-performance scenarios. Understand GC generations, allocations, and large object heap (LOH).

Memory Management Concepts:
1. Stack vs Heap: Value types on stack (or inline), reference types on heap
2. GC Generations: Gen 0 (young), Gen 1 (intermediate), Gen 2 (old), LOH (large objects >85KB)
3. GC Modes: Workstation (concurrent), Server (parallel), ephemeral segments
4. Pinning: Prevent GC moving objects (needed for interop)

Resource Management:
• IDisposable Pattern: Standard way to release unmanaged resources
• SafeHandle: Wrapper for operating system handles
• Using Statements: Automatic disposal with scope
• Finalizers: Last-chance cleanup (avoid if possible)

Performance Optimization:
• Span<T>/Memory<T>: View over memory without copying
• ArrayPool<T>: Reuse arrays to reduce allocations
• Stackalloc: Allocate on stack for small arrays
• ValueTask<T>: Reduce allocations for async methods
• Structs: Avoid heap allocation for small data

interview.q: How to release unmanaged resources properly?
interview.a: 1) Implement IDisposable interface, 2) Use SafeHandle for operating system handles, 3) Implement Dispose pattern with protected virtual Dispose(bool), 4) Call GC.SuppressFinalize(this), 5) Provide finalizer as backup, 6) Use 'using' statements for deterministic cleanup, 7) For derived classes, chain Dispose calls, 8) Make type immutable to prevent disposed object reuse.

interview.q: What is Span<T> and when to use it?
interview.a: Span<T> is a stack-only ref struct providing view over contiguous memory (arrays, strings, stackalloc, unmanaged). Use for: 1) High-performance parsing/processing without allocations, 2) Slicing collections without copying, 3) Interop scenarios, 4) Algorithm implementations working on memory ranges. Limitations: Cannot be stored on heap, cannot be used in async methods or as class fields.

interview.q: Explain the .NET garbage collection algorithm
interview.a: .NET uses generational mark-and-compact GC. Three generations: Gen 0 (young objects, collected frequently), Gen 1 (buffer between), Gen 2 (long-lived objects, collected rarely). LOH for objects >85KB. Algorithm: 1) Mark reachable objects starting from roots, 2) Sweep unreachable objects, 3) Compact memory to reduce fragmentation. Uses ephemeral segments for Gen 0/1.

interview.q: What is the difference between 'using' statement and 'using' declaration?
interview.a: 'using' statement (block-based): Resource disposed at end of block. 'using' declaration (C# 8+): Resource disposed at end of enclosing scope (method). Declaration form reduces nesting, improves readability. Both compile to try-finally blocks. Declaration can't be used when you need different disposal scope than method scope.

interview.q: When should you implement a finalizer?
interview.a: Implement finalizer only when class directly owns unmanaged resources. Avoid if possible. If needed: 1) Implement with ~ClassName(), 2) Call Dispose(false) from finalizer, 3) Only release unmanaged resources in finalizer, 4) Use CriticalFinalizerObject for guaranteed execution. Finalizers have performance cost and execution timing is non-deterministic.

interview.q: What is the Large Object Heap (LOH) and its implications?
interview.a: LOH stores objects >85KB. Characteristics: 1) Collected only during Gen 2 collections, 2) Not compacted by default (causes fragmentation), 3) Can cause OutOfMemory even with free memory. Mitigations: 1) Use ArrayPool for large arrays, 2) Use streaming for large data, 3) In .NET Core 3.0+, LOH compaction can be enabled with GCSettings.LargeObjectHeapCompactionMode.

interview.q: How does ArrayPool<T> improve performance?
interview.a: ArrayPool rents and returns arrays from shared pool, reducing: 1) GC pressure from frequent allocations, 2) Memory fragmentation, 3) Time spent in GC. Use for temporary buffers in performance-critical code. Always return arrays to pool. Configure pooling behavior with ArrayPool<T>.Create() for custom pools.

interview.q: Explain the difference between Span<T> and Memory<T>
interview.a: Span<T> is ref struct (stack-only), cannot be stored on heap, used for synchronous code. Memory<T> is regular struct, can be stored on heap, used for asynchronous code. Both provide view over memory. Use Span for stack-based operations, Memory for async operations or when storage in fields is needed. Convert between with .Span and .Memory properties.

interview.q: What is pinning and when is it necessary?
interview.a: Pinning prevents GC from moving object during compaction. Necessary when: 1) Passing managed buffers to native code, 2) Using pointers in unsafe code, 3) Interop scenarios requiring fixed addresses. Use 'fixed' statement or GCHandle.Alloc(). Pinning reduces GC efficiency and can cause fragmentation - minimize duration and frequency.

interview.q: How do you diagnose memory leaks in .NET?
interview.a: Tools: 1) Visual Studio Diagnostic Tools, 2) dotMemory, 3) PerfView, 4) .NET Object Allocation Tracker. Techniques: 1) Analyze GC roots, 2) Check event handler subscriptions, 3) Review static collections, 4) Examine finalization queue, 5) Use WeakReference for caches, 6) Check for disposed objects still referenced. Common causes: event handlers, caches, static collections, timers.

interview.q: What is the difference between stackalloc and regular array allocation?
interview.a: stackalloc allocates on stack: 1) Very fast (pointer adjustment), 2) Limited size (~1MB default), 3) Auto-cleaned when method returns, 4) No GC overhead. Regular arrays allocate on heap: 1) Managed by GC, 2) Larger size possible, 3) Can cause allocations/fragmentation. Use stackalloc for small, temporary buffers in performance-critical code.

interview.q: When should you call GC.Collect()?
interview.a: Rarely. Consider calling when: 1) Application enters idle state, 2) After disposing many objects, 3) In performance tests to establish baseline, 4) When using unmanaged memory pressure. Avoid in production code - GC is self-tuning. If frequent collections needed, fix allocation patterns instead.

interview.q: Explain the IDisposable pattern for derived classes
interview.a: In derived classes: 1) Override Dispose(bool) method, 2) Call base.Dispose(disposing), 3) Add your own managed/unmanaged resources, 4) Don't override Dispose() method. Pattern ensures proper chaining. Sealed classes can implement simplified pattern without virtual method.

interview.q: What are WeakReference and WeakEvent patterns?
interview.a: WeakReference allows reference to object without preventing GC collection. Use for: 1) Caches that shouldn't keep objects alive, 2) Observer patterns to prevent memory leaks. WeakEvent pattern uses weak references for event handlers, allowing subscribers to be collected. Implement with WeakEventManager or custom weak event patterns.

interview.q: How does async/await affect memory management?
interview.a: Async state machines allocate on heap for non-completed tasks. Optimizations: 1) Use ValueTask<T> for synchronous completion, 2) Pool buffers with ArrayPool, 3) Avoid closure allocations with static/local functions, 4) Use IValueTaskSource for advanced scenarios. Async streams (IAsyncEnumerable) can allocate enumerators - consider pooling.

interview.q: What is memory fragmentation and how to avoid it?
interview.a: Fragmentation occurs when free memory is scattered in small blocks. Causes: 1) Pinned objects, 2) LOH allocations, 3) Mixing object sizes. Avoid by: 1) Using ArrayPool for buffers, 2) Minimizing pinning duration, 3) Using structs for small objects, 4) Pre-allocating large buffers, 5) Using memory-mapped files for large data.

interview.q: Explain the using statement's implementation
interview.a: 'using' compiles to try-finally block: 'using (var r = new Resource()) { }' becomes 'Resource r = new Resource(); try { } finally { if (r != null) ((IDisposable)r).Dispose(); }'. For async using: 'await using' uses try-finally with await in finally. Declaration form adds hidden try-finally at scope end.

interview.q: What is SafeHandle and why is it preferred?
interview.a: SafeHandle is abstract class wrapping operating system handles. Benefits: 1) Guaranteed cleanup even during AppDomain unload, 2) Prevention of handle recycling attacks, 3) Simplified finalizer implementation, 4) Interop safety. Derived classes: SafeFileHandle, SafeWaitHandle. Use instead of IntPtr for handles.

interview.q: How do you optimize memory usage for collections?
interview.a: 1) Specify initial capacity for List/Dictionary, 2) Use arrays for fixed-size collections, 3) Consider ArrayPool for temporary buffers, 4) Use structs for small elements, 5) Implement custom collections with pooling, 6) Use Memory<T>/Span<T> APIs, 7) Consider frozen collections (.NET 8+) for immutable data.

interview.q: What is the difference between GC.GetTotalMemory(false) and GC.GetTotalMemory(true)?
interview.a: GC.GetTotalMemory(false) returns current allocated bytes without forcing collection. GC.GetTotalMemory(true) forces Gen 2 collection before measurement, giving more accurate picture of live objects. Use false for monitoring, true for precise measurements. Both are estimates due to GC overhead and fragmentation.

csharp_async.title: Async/Await & Tasks
csharp_async.summary: Task-based async model, async/await, Task vs ValueTask, cancellation with CancellationToken.
csharp_async.details:
Use async/await to write asynchronous code. Prefer ConfigureAwait(false) in libraries, propagate CancellationToken, and maintain responsiveness. Use ValueTask to reduce allocations in hot paths where appropriate. Understand synchronization context differences (UI vs threadpool).

Async/Await Architecture:
1. State Machines: Compiler transforms async methods into state machines
2. SynchronizationContext: Captures context for UI threads, null for thread pool
3. Task Scheduler: Controls how and where tasks execute
4. Task Continuations: How tasks chain together

Key Patterns:
• Exception Handling: Exceptions captured in Task, rethrown on await
• Task Composition: Task.WhenAll, Task.WhenAny, Task.Delay
• Cancellation Patterns: CancellationTokenSource, linked tokens, timeout patterns
• Progress Reporting: IProgress<T> for UI updates
• Async Streams: IAsyncEnumerable<T> for async sequence processing

Performance Considerations:
• Task Allocation Overhead: Each Task allocates ~300 bytes on heap
• ValueTask Optimization: Stack-based allocation for synchronous completion
• ConfigureAwait: Context capture overhead
• Async State Machine: Small overhead per async method

interview.q: What is ConfigureAwait(false) and when should you use it?
interview.a: ConfigureAwait(false) prevents capturing the SynchronizationContext, allowing continuation to run on any thread pool thread. Use it in library code to: 1) Improve performance (avoid context capture overhead), 2) Prevent deadlocks in UI applications, 3) Allow parallelism. Don't use it when you need to return to original context (e.g., UI updates).

interview.q: When should you use ValueTask vs Task?
interview.a: Use ValueTask when: 1) Method often completes synchronously (caching, validation), 2) You're writing performance-critical libraries, 3) Allocating Task would cause measurable overhead. Use Task for: 1) Most async methods, 2) Public APIs where consumers expect Task, 3) When result may be awaited multiple times. ValueTask has restrictions: can't be awaited multiple times unless you know it's backed by Task.

interview.q: How does async/await work under the hood?
interview.a: Compiler transforms async method into state machine class with: 1) MoveNext() method tracking execution, 2) Builder (AsyncTaskMethodBuilder) managing Task, 3) Local variables become fields. On await: 1) Checks if operation completed, 2) If not, returns incomplete Task, 3) Registers continuation. SynchronizationContext determines where continuation runs. Exceptions are captured in Task.

interview.q: What is the difference between Task.Run and async/await?
interview.a: Task.Run queues work to thread pool, useful for CPU-bound operations. async/await is for I/O-bound operations - doesn't tie up threads while waiting. Task.Run + async = offloading to thread pool then async wait. Use Task.Run to make synchronous code async, but prefer true async APIs when available.

interview.q: How do you handle exceptions in async methods?
interview.a: Exceptions in async methods are captured in returned Task. Awaiting the Task rethrows the exception. Use try/catch around await. Multiple exceptions from Task.WhenAll are wrapped in AggregateException. For fire-and-forget tasks, attach continuation to handle exceptions. Always handle OperationCanceledException for cancellations.

interview.q: What is the difference between async void and async Task?
interview.a: async void: Can't be awaited, exceptions crash process (fire-and-forget). Use only for event handlers. async Task: Can be awaited, exceptions captured in Task. Always prefer async Task over async void. async Task<T> returns result. async void makes error handling difficult and testing impossible.

interview.q: How do CancellationToken and CancellationTokenSource work?
interview.a: CancellationToken is struct passed to async methods to monitor cancellation. CancellationTokenSource creates tokens and signals cancellation. Patterns: 1) Pass token to async methods, 2) Check token.IsCancellationRequested, 3) Call token.ThrowIfCancellationRequested(), 4) Use CreateLinkedTokenSource for multiple sources, 5) CancelAfter for timeouts.

interview.q: What are async streams (IAsyncEnumerable) and when to use them?
interview.a: IAsyncEnumerable<T> represents async sequence of values. Use when: 1) Producing sequence with async operations, 2) Processing large datasets from async sources, 3) Real-time data streaming. Consume with await foreach. Supports cancellation via EnumeratorCancellation attribute. More efficient than returning List<T> for large async sequences.

interview.q: How do you prevent deadlocks with async/await?
interview.a: Deadlocks occur when: 1) Blocking on async code (.Result, .Wait()), 2) SynchronizationContext deadlock. Prevent by: 1) Use async all the way, 2) Use ConfigureAwait(false) in library code, 3) Don't block on async code, 4) Use Task.Run to offload if necessary, 5) Use SemaphoreSlim.WaitAsync for async locks.

interview.q: What is TaskCompletionSource and when to use it?
interview.a: TaskCompletionSource creates Task you can manually control. Use to: 1) Wrap event-based APIs in Task, 2) Create tasks that complete based on external events, 3) Implement async wrappers for synchronous code, 4) Create cancellable tasks. Call SetResult, SetException, or SetCanceled to complete the Task.

interview.q: How do you implement retry logic with async/await?
interview.a: Use Polly library or implement: exponential backoff, circuit breaker. Manual implementation: loop with try/catch, delay with Task.Delay, cancellation support, max retries. Consider: transient error detection, jitter to prevent thundering herd, logging retries. Use async/await for non-blocking delays between retries.

interview.q: What is the difference between WhenAll and WhenAny?
interview.a: Task.WhenAll: Waits for all tasks to complete, returns array of results. If any fail, throws AggregateException. Task.WhenAny: Waits for first task to complete, returns completed task. Useful for: timeouts, racing multiple services, progress reporting. WhenAny doesn't cancel other tasks - handle cancellation separately.

interview.q: How do you implement async lazy initialization?
interview.a: Use AsyncLazy<T> pattern or Lazy<Task<T>>. Lazy<Task<T>> ensures single execution and caching. AsyncLazy adds cancellation support. Implementation: lock/semaphore for thread safety, TaskCompletionSource for async completion. .NET 6+ has Lazy<T> with value factory, but not async-aware.

interview.q: What are the performance implications of async/await?
interview.a: Overheads: 1) State machine allocation (~100 bytes), 2) Task allocation (~300 bytes), 3) Context switch if not completed synchronously. Optimizations: 1) ValueTask for sync completion, 2) ConfigureAwait(false), 3) Cache tasks (Task.CompletedTask), 4) Pool objects, 5) Avoid async for trivial methods. Benefits outweigh costs for I/O.

interview.q: How do you write async unit tests?
interview.a: Test methods can be async Task. Use testing frameworks that support async (xUnit, NUnit, MSTest). Test cancellation with CancellationTokenSource. Mock async methods using Task.FromResult or TaskCompletionSource. Test timeouts with Task.Delay and cancellation. Use Assert.ThrowsAsync for exception testing.

interview.q: What is SynchronizationContext and how does it affect async?
interview.a: SynchronizationContext provides abstraction for posting work to specific context (UI thread, ASP.NET request context). async captures current context unless ConfigureAwait(false). UI apps have DispatcherSynchronizationContext, ASP.NET has AspNetSynchronizationContext (removed in Core). Understanding context prevents deadlocks and performance issues.

interview.q: How do you implement async locks?
interview.a: Use SemaphoreSlim with WaitAsync() for async locking. Avoid Monitor/lock with async. AsyncReaderWriterLock for read/write scenarios. Implement with TaskCompletionSource for custom locks. Consider if locking is needed - often async code needs less locking due to non-blocking nature.

interview.q: What are the best practices for async API design?
interview.a: 1) Use CancellationToken parameter, 2) Use ConfigureAwait(false) in library code, 3) Prefer Task<T> over void, 4) Use async suffix consistently, 5) Document cancellation behavior, 6) Provide sync alternatives if needed, 7) Use IAsyncDisposable for resources, 8) Consider IAsyncEnumerable for sequences.

interview.q: How do you handle multiple async operations with dependencies?
interview.a: Chain with await: var a = await A(); var b = await B(a);. For parallel independent operations: Task.WhenAll. For complex workflows: consider Dataflow library or async state machines. Use continuation tasks (ContinueWith) carefully - prefer await for readability. Implement async pipelines with functional composition.

interview.q: What is the difference between Task.Delay and Thread.Sleep?
interview.a: Task.Delay is async - doesn't block thread, returns Task that completes after delay. Thread.Sleep blocks current thread. Use Task.Delay in async code for non-blocking delays. Thread.Sleep in sync code or tests. Task.Delay supports cancellation via CancellationToken. Task.Delay uses timer queue, Thread.Sleep uses OS scheduler.

csharp_linq.title: LINQ & Functional Patterns
csharp_linq.summary: LINQ queries, deferred execution, IQueryable vs IEnumerable and projection/filtering.
csharp_linq.details:
LINQ provides a declarative API for querying collections. Remember deferred execution, side effects in queries, and how LINQ to Entities translates to SQL when used with EF Core. Use Select/Where/Aggregate and be mindful of performance in large datasets.

LINQ Architecture:
1. Extension Methods: LINQ methods are extension methods on IEnumerable<T> and IQueryable<T>
2. Expression Trees: IQueryable uses expression trees for query translation
3. Deferred Execution: Queries execute when enumerated, not when defined
4. Lazy vs Eager Evaluation: Understanding when queries execute

LINQ Providers:
• LINQ to Objects: In-memory collections (IEnumerable<T>)
• LINQ to Entities/SQL: Database queries via EF Core (IQueryable<T>)
• LINQ to XML: XML document querying
• Parallel LINQ (PLINQ): Parallel query execution

Functional Patterns:
• Higher-Order Functions: Methods that take or return functions (Select, Where)
• Immutability: LINQ creates new sequences, doesn't modify source
• Composition: Chain methods to build complex queries
• Pure Functions: Avoid side effects in query expressions

interview.q: What is deferred execution in LINQ and why is it important?
interview.a: Deferred execution means LINQ queries are not executed when defined, but when enumerated (via foreach, ToList(), etc.). Important because: 1) Allows query composition, 2) Avoids unnecessary work if query is never used, 3) Enables query optimization. Operators like Where, Select, OrderBy are deferred; ToList, Count, Average cause immediate execution.

interview.q: What is the difference between IQueryable and IEnumerable?
interview.a: IEnumerable<T> works with in-memory collections, uses delegates (Func<T, bool>). IQueryable<T> works with remote data sources, uses expression trees (Expression<Func<T, bool>>) that can be translated (e.g., to SQL). IQueryable builds queries that execute on server; IEnumerable executes on client. Use IQueryable with databases, IEnumerable with local collections.

interview.q: How does LINQ to SQL/Entities translate queries to SQL?
interview.a: LINQ providers analyze expression trees from IQueryable, converting them to provider-specific queries (SQL for EF Core). Translation includes: method calls to SQL functions, joins to SQL JOINs, predicates to WHERE clauses. Some C# constructs can't be translated (client evaluation). Use AsEnumerable() to switch to client evaluation when needed.

interview.q: What are the performance considerations with LINQ?
interview.a: 1) Deferred execution causing multiple enumerations, 2) N+1 queries with lazy loading, 3) Client evaluation vs server evaluation, 4) Large object allocations for anonymous types, 5) Multiple Where() vs single complex predicate, 6) Use Any() not Count() > 0, 7) Materialize queries for reuse, 8) Consider PLINQ for CPU-bound operations.

interview.q: Explain the difference between First() and Single()
interview.a: First() returns first matching element; throws if sequence empty. Single() returns the only matching element; throws if zero or multiple matches. Use First() when you expect/allow multiple matches. Use Single() when you expect exactly one match (e.g., by primary key). FirstOrDefault()/SingleOrDefault() return default instead of throwing.

interview.q: How do you handle null values in LINQ queries?
interview.a: 1) Use null-conditional operator (?.), 2) Use DefaultIfEmpty() for left joins, 3) Use ?? operator for defaults, 4) Filter nulls with Where(x => x != null), 5) Use OfType<T>() to filter by type and remove nulls, 6) Be careful with string comparisons (StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase). Consider nullable reference types for compile-time checking.

interview.q: What is the difference between Select and SelectMany?
interview.a: Select transforms each element (1-to-1). SelectMany flattens collections (1-to-many). Example: Select on list of orders gives list of order amounts. SelectMany on list of customers with orders gives flat list of all orders. SelectMany is used for joins and flattening nested collections.

interview.q: How do you implement custom LINQ operators?
interview.a: Create extension methods on IEnumerable<T> or IQueryable<T>. For IEnumerable: accept Func delegates. For IQueryable: accept Expression<Func<>> to support query translation. Use yield return for deferred execution. Consider implementing both sync and async versions. Follow LINQ naming conventions (prefix with To for materialization).

interview.q: What are expression trees and how do they relate to LINQ?
interview.a: Expression trees are code-as-data representations (System.Linq.Expressions). IQueryable uses expression trees so providers can analyze/translate queries. Expressions can be compiled to delegates at runtime. Used for: 1) LINQ to SQL translation, 2) Dynamic query building, 3) Reflection alternatives, 4) Code generation. Expressions are immutable; modify via ExpressionVisitor.

interview.q: How do you handle large datasets with LINQ?
interview.a: 1) Use streaming with yield return, 2) Implement paging with Skip/Take, 3) Use database-side filtering with IQueryable, 4) Consider async streams (IAsyncEnumerable), 5) Use chunking for batch processing, 6) Avoid materializing entire dataset, 7) Use PLINQ for parallel processing of in-memory data, 8) Consider database-specific optimizations.

interview.q: What is the difference between Join and GroupJoin?
interview.a: Join performs inner join - pairs matching elements. GroupJoin performs left join grouping - for each left element, groups matching right elements. Join produces flat result. GroupJoin produces hierarchical result. Use Join for 1-to-1 relationships, GroupJoin for 1-to-many. Left join can be achieved with GroupJoin + DefaultIfEmpty.

interview.q: How do you debug LINQ queries?
interview.a: 1) Materialize intermediate results with ToList() to inspect, 2) Use debugger visualizers, 3) Log generated SQL with EF Core logging, 4) Use LINQPad for query testing, 5) Break complex queries into steps, 6) Use Select to add debugging side effects, 7) Check for client vs server evaluation warnings, 8) Profile query performance.

interview.q: What are the differences between query syntax and method syntax?
interview.a: Query syntax uses SQL-like keywords (from, where, select). Method syntax uses extension methods (Where, Select). Query syntax is often more readable for complex queries (joins, grouping). Method syntax is more flexible (custom extension methods). Some operations only available in method syntax (Skip, Take, Aggregate). They compile to the same IL.

interview.q: How do you implement dynamic LINQ queries?
interview.a: 1) Build expression trees manually, 2) Use System.Linq.Dynamic.Core library, 3) Use predicate builder patterns, 4) Chain Where conditions conditionally, 5) Use IQueryable composition. Example: start with base query, add filters based on conditions. Be careful with SQL injection if using string-based dynamic LINQ.

interview.q: What is PLINQ and when should you use it?
interview.a: Parallel LINQ extends LINQ with parallel execution via AsParallel(). Use for CPU-bound operations on large in-memory collections. Considerations: 1) Overhead of parallelization, 2) Thread safety of operations, 3) Order preservation with AsOrdered(), 4) Degree of parallelism control. Not suitable for I/O-bound operations or small collections.

interview.q: How do you handle exceptions in LINQ queries?
interview.a: 1) Validate data before querying, 2) Use Try-catch around materialization (ToList()), 3) Handle specific exceptions (InvalidOperationException for First on empty), 4) Use *OrDefault methods, 5) Consider fallback values with DefaultIfEmpty, 6) Log query failures, 7) Test edge cases (null collections, empty results).

interview.q: What are the common pitfalls with LINQ?
interview.a: 1) Multiple enumeration causing repeated work, 2) Client evaluation of large datasets, 3) N+1 query problem, 4) Capturing variables in closures, 5) Side effects in query expressions, 6) Assuming query execution order, 7) Not disposing of data contexts, 8) Ignoring null reference warnings, 9) Using Count() instead of Any().

interview.q: How do you optimize LINQ for database queries?
interview.a: 1) Use IQueryable for server-side execution, 2) Eager load related data with Include(), 3) Use projection (Select) to limit columns, 4) Implement proper indexing, 5) Use compiled queries for frequent queries, 6) Batch operations, 7) Monitor generated SQL, 8) Use asynchronous operations, 9) Implement query caching where appropriate.

interview.q: What is the difference between ToList() and ToArray()?
interview.a: Both materialize queries. ToList() returns List<T> (resizable). ToArray() returns T[] (fixed size). List has better performance for adding elements. Array has slightly better memory layout for iteration. Use ToList() when you need to modify collection. Use ToArray() when you need array or immutable collection. ToList() often faster as it doesn't need to know final size upfront.

interview.q: How do you implement pagination with LINQ?
interview.a: Use Skip() and Take(): var page = query.Skip(pageNumber * pageSize).Take(pageSize).ToList(). For efficient database paging: 1) Use ordered queries, 2) Consider keyset pagination for large datasets, 3) Get total count separately, 4) Use IQueryable for server-side paging. For web APIs, include metadata (total pages, next page URL).

csharp_collections.title: Collections & Generics
csharp_collections.summary: List/Dictionary/HashSet, concurrent collections, and choosing the right collection for complexity and concurrency.
csharp_collections.details:
Use generic collections (List<T>, Dictionary<TKey,TValue>). For concurrent scenarios, use ConcurrentDictionary/ConcurrentQueue. Understand complexity characteristics and memory trade-offs.

Collection Categories:
1. Generic Collections: List<T>, Dictionary<TKey,TValue>, HashSet<T>, Stack<T>, Queue<T>
2. Concurrent Collections: Thread-safe alternatives in System.Collections.Concurrent
3. Immutable Collections: Thread-safe, immutable collections in System.Collections.Immutable
4. Specialized Collections: BitArray, StringCollection, HybridDictionary

Performance Characteristics:
• List<T>: O(1) access by index, O(n) insert/remove (except at end)
• Dictionary<TKey,TValue>: O(1) average, O(n) worst case for lookup
• HashSet<T>: O(1) average for membership test
• Sorted Collections: O(log n) operations but maintain order

Memory Considerations:
• Collection overhead per element
• Capacity vs Count trade-offs
• Boxing/Unboxing with non-generic collections
• Array pooling for temporary collections

interview.q: When to use Dictionary vs SortedDictionary vs SortedList?
interview.a: Dictionary<TKey,TValue>: Use for O(1) lookups when order doesn't matter. SortedDictionary<TKey,TValue>: Red-black tree, O(log n) operations, maintains sorted order, good for frequent inserts/deletes. SortedList<TKey,TValue>: Array-based, O(log n) lookup, O(n) insert/delete, memory efficient for read-heavy, sorted data. Choose based on operation frequency and memory constraints.

interview.q: What are concurrent collections and when to use them?
interview.a: Concurrent collections in System.Collections.Concurrent provide thread-safe operations without external locking. Use when multiple threads access the same collection. ConcurrentDictionary for key-value pairs, ConcurrentQueue for FIFO, ConcurrentStack for LIFO, ConcurrentBag for unordered items, BlockingCollection for producer-consumer with bounding. They use fine-grained locking and atomic operations for performance.

interview.q: Explain the differences between List<T>, HashSet<T>, and Dictionary<TKey,TValue>
interview.a: List<T>: Ordered collection, allows duplicates, O(1) index access, O(n) search. HashSet<T>: Unordered unique elements, O(1) membership test, no indexing. Dictionary<TKey,TValue>: Key-value pairs, unique keys, O(1) key lookup. Use List for ordered sequences, HashSet for unique sets, Dictionary for key-based lookups.

interview.q: What are immutable collections and their advantages?
interview.a: Immutable collections in System.Collections.Immutable cannot be modified after creation. Advantages: 1) Thread-safe by design, 2) Versioning support, 3) Functional programming patterns, 4) Safe sharing between components. Modifications return new collections. Slightly slower than mutable collections but enable safe concurrency and predictable state.

interview.q: How do you choose between array and List<T>?
interview.a: Use arrays when: 1) Size is fixed and known, 2) Maximum performance needed, 3) Interop with native code, 4) Multi-dimensional data. Use List<T> when: 1) Size changes dynamically, 2) Need Add/Insert/Remove methods, 3) Working with LINQ, 4) Code readability preferred. List<T> uses array internally but handles resizing.

interview.q: What is the difference between ICollection<T>, IList<T>, and IEnumerable<T>?
interview.a: IEnumerable<T>: Basic iteration, single method GetEnumerator(). ICollection<T>: Adds Count, Add, Remove, Clear, Contains. IList<T>: Adds indexer, Insert, RemoveAt, IndexOf. Choose minimal interface needed: IEnumerable for readonly iteration, ICollection for modifiable collections without indexing, IList for indexed access.

interview.q: How does Dictionary<TKey,TValue> handle collisions?
interview.a: Dictionary uses chaining (linked lists) for collisions. Each bucket contains linked list of entries. Hash function distributes keys, collisions resolved by traversing chain. Load factor triggers resizing (default 0.72). Good hash function minimizes collisions. Custom IEqualityComparer can be provided for complex keys.

interview.q: What are the performance characteristics of common collections?
interview.a: List: Index access O(1), search O(n), insert/delete O(n) except end O(1). Dictionary: Lookup O(1) average, O(n) worst, insert/delete O(1) average. HashSet: Contains O(1) average. SortedDictionary: All operations O(log n). LinkedList: Insert/delete at known position O(1), search O(n). Choose based on operation frequencies.

interview.q: How do you implement custom collection classes?
interview.a: 1) Implement IEnumerable<T> for iteration, 2) Consider ICollection<T> for standard operations, 3) Use composition (contain existing collection), 4) Override appropriate methods, 5) Consider implementing sync/async versions, 6) Provide appropriate constructors, 7) Implement equality comparison. Use Collection<T> or KeyedCollection<TKey,TItem> as base classes for common scenarios.

interview.q: What is BlockingCollection<T> and how is it used?
interview.a: BlockingCollection<T> is a thread-safe collection with bounding and blocking operations. Used for producer-consumer patterns. Methods: Add() blocks if bounded capacity reached, Take() blocks if empty. Supports multiple producers/consumers. Can wrap other concurrent collections. Use CompleteAdding() to signal no more items.

interview.q: Explain covariance and contravariance in generic collections
interview.a: Covariance (out): IEnumerable<Derived> can be assigned to IEnumerable<Base>. Contravariance (in): IComparer<Base> can be assigned to IComparer<Derived>. Enables more flexible type assignments. Supported in: IEnumerable<out T>, IEnumerator<out T> (covariant), IComparer<in T> (contravariant). Requires interface declaration with 'out' or 'in' modifiers.

interview.q: How do you optimize collection performance?
interview.a: 1) Pre-allocate capacity when size known, 2) Use right collection for operation patterns, 3) Specify initial capacity for dictionaries, 4) Use structs for small elements to reduce heap allocations, 5) Pool collections for reuse, 6) Use Span<T> for temporary operations, 7) Consider ArrayPool for large arrays, 8) Avoid boxing with generics.

interview.q: What are the differences between Queue<T> and ConcurrentQueue<T>?
interview.a: Queue<T> is not thread-safe, requires external synchronization for multi-threaded access. ConcurrentQueue<T> provides thread-safe Enqueue and TryDequeue methods using atomic operations and fine-grained locking. ConcurrentQueue is slower for single-threaded scenarios due to synchronization overhead. Use Queue for single-threaded, ConcurrentQueue for multi-threaded.

interview.q: How do you handle null values in collections?
interview.a: 1) Check for null before adding, 2) Use nullable reference types (C# 8+), 3) Allow nulls if collection supports it (List<T> allows), 4) Dictionary doesn't allow null keys but allows null values, 5) HashSet<T> doesn't allow null (throws), 6) Use default values or Option pattern. Document null handling in API contracts.

interview.q: What are linked lists and when to use them?
interview.a: LinkedList<T> stores elements in nodes with forward/backward references. Advantages: O(1) insert/delete at known position, no capacity resizing. Disadvantages: O(n) access by index, memory overhead per node. Use when: 1) Frequent insertions/deletions in middle, 2) Implementing queues/stacks, 3) Maintaining order with frequent modifications.

interview.q: How do you serialize collections?
interview.a: Most collections implement ISerializable. Use: 1) [Serializable] attribute, 2) DataContractSerializer for WCF, 3) JsonSerializer for JSON, 4) XmlSerializer for XML. Considerations: circular references, version tolerance, custom serialization. For dictionaries, ensure key type is serializable. Use collection interfaces in serialized contracts for flexibility.

interview.q: What are collection initializers and how do they work?
interview.a: Collection initializers allow initialization syntax: new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 }. Compiler translates to Add() calls. Requirements: Type implements IEnumerable, has public Add() method. Works with dictionaries: new Dictionary<string, int> { {"a", 1}, {"b", 2} }. Custom collections can support by implementing Add() method.

interview.q: How do you compare collections for equality?
interview.a: Use SequenceEqual() for ordered equality (same elements in same order). For unordered: convert to HashSet and use SetEquals(). For dictionaries: compare key-value pairs. Implement IEqualityComparer<T> for custom comparisons. Consider overriding Equals() and GetHashCode() for collection classes.

interview.q: What are the memory considerations for large collections?
interview.a: 1) Each collection has overhead (List ~24 bytes + array), 2) Dictionary overhead higher due to buckets, 3) Consider Large Object Heap (>85KB), 4) Use capacity management to reduce resizing, 5) Consider streaming for very large data, 6) Use structs to reduce heap pressure, 7) Pool collections, 8) Use weak references for caches.

interview.q: How do you implement a thread-safe cache?
interview.a: Use ConcurrentDictionary for basic cache. For LRU cache: combine ConcurrentDictionary with LinkedList for ordering. Consider MemoryCache for feature-rich caching. Implement expiration policies, size limits, and cleanup. Use ReaderWriterLockSlim for read-heavy caches. Consider distributed caches (Redis) for multi-instance applications.

csharp_dotnet.title: .NET, SDK & Tooling
csharp_dotnet.summary: dotnet CLI, SDK versions, project files (.csproj), and global.json for SDK pinning.
csharp_dotnet.details:
Use dotnet CLI for projects, manage SDK with global.json, and understand target frameworks (net6.0, net7.0). Use NuGet for packages and integrate with CI. Familiarize with msbuild properties and multi-targeting.

.NET Ecosystem Components:
1. Runtime: CLR (Common Language Runtime) executes managed code
2. SDK: Development tools, compilers, libraries
3. Target Frameworks: net6.0, net7.0, net8.0, netstandard2.0, etc.
4. Project Types: Console, Web API, Class Library, Blazor, MAUI

Tooling Features:
• dotnet CLI: Command-line interface for all operations
• Visual Studio: Full IDE with advanced features
• VS Code: Lightweight editor with C# Dev Kit
• Rider: Cross-platform IDE from JetBrains
• MSBuild: Build engine with project files

// DOTNET CLI COMMANDS
// Project management
dotnet new console -n MyApp  // Create console app
dotnet new webapi -n MyApi   // Create Web API
dotnet new classlib -n MyLib // Create class library

dotnet sln add MyApp         // Add project to solution
dotnet sln list              // List solution projects

// Building and running
dotnet build                 // Build project
dotnet build --configuration Release  // Release build
dotnet run                   // Build and run
dotnet run --no-build        // Run without building
dotnet run --project MyApp   // Run specific project

dotnet clean                 // Clean build artifacts

dotnet publish               // Publish for deployment
dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 --self-contained

// Testing
dotnet test                  // Run all tests
dotnet test --filter "Category=Integration"
dotnet test MyTestProject    // Run tests for specific project

dotnet vstest *.dll          // Run tests using VSTest

// Package management
dotnet add package Newtonsoft.Json        // Add package
dotnet add package Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.SqlServer --version 7.0.0
dotnet remove package Newtonsoft.Json    // Remove package
dotnet list package                       // List packages
dotnet restore                            // Restore packages

dotnet pack                               // Create NuGet package
dotnet nuget push MyPackage.1.0.0.nupkg --api-key xxx --source https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json

// Tool management
dotnet tool install -g dotnet-ef          // Install global tool
dotnet tool update -g dotnet-ef          // Update tool
dotnet tool list -g                      // List global tools
dotnet ef migrations add InitialCreate   // Use EF tool

// Code quality
dotnet format                             // Format code
dotnet format --verify-no-changes        // Check formatting
dotnet format whitespace                 // Format whitespace only

dotnet analyze                            // Run code analysis
dotnet csharpier                          // Format with CSharpier

// Diagnostics
dotnet --info                             // Show .NET info
dotnet --version                          // Show SDK version
dotnet --list-sdks                        // List installed SDKs
dotnet --list-runtimes                    // List installed runtimes

dotnet ef database update                 // Update database

dotnet workload install maui             // Install workload

dotnet dev-certs https --trust           // Trust HTTPS cert

// PROJECT FILE (.csproj) EXAMPLES
// Basic console app
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

// Web API with specific settings
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <InvariantGlobalization>true</InvariantGlobalization>
  </PropertyGroup>
  
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.SqlServer" Version="8.0.0" />
    <PackageReference Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

// Multi-targeting library
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFrameworks>net8.0;net7.0;net6.0;netstandard2.1</TargetFrameworks>
    <GeneratePackageOnBuild>true</GeneratePackageOnBuild>
    <Version>1.0.0</Version>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

// GLOBAL.JSON for SDK pinning
{
  "sdk": {
    "version": "8.0.100",
    "rollForward": "disable",
    "allowPrerelease": false
  }
}

// COMMON MSBUILD PROPERTIES
<PropertyGroup>
  <!-- Output -->
  <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
  <OutputPath>bin\$(Configuration)</OutputPath>
  <AssemblyName>MyApp</AssemblyName>
  
  <!-- Compilation -->
  <LangVersion>latest</LangVersion>
  <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
  <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
  <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
  
  <!-- Debugging -->
  <DebugType>portable</DebugType>
  <DebugSymbols>true</DebugSymbols>
  
  <!-- Optimization -->
  <Optimize>true</Optimize>
  <Deterministic>true</Deterministic>
  
  <!-- Package -->
  <PackageId>MyCompany.MyPackage</PackageId>
  <Version>1.0.0</Version>
  <Authors>Your Name</Authors>
  <Company>Your Company</Company>
  <Description>Package description</Description>
</PropertyGroup>

// CONDITIONAL PROPERTIES
<PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Release'">
  <Optimize>true</Optimize>
  <DebugType>none</DebugType>
</PropertyGroup>

// ITEM GROUPS
<ItemGroup>
  <!-- Package references -->
  <PackageReference Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />
  
  <!-- Project references -->
  <ProjectReference Include="..\MyLib\MyLib.csproj" />
  
  <!-- Content files -->
  <Content Include="appsettings.json">
    <CopyToOutputDirectory>PreserveNewest</CopyToOutputDirectory>
  </Content>
  
  <!-- Compile includes/excludes -->
  <Compile Include="**\*.cs" Exclude="**\*.test.cs" />
</ItemGroup>
