interview.q: How to pin SDK version in a project?
interview.a: Create global.json file in solution root with SDK version: {"sdk": {"version": "8.0.100", "rollForward": "disable"}}. rollForward options: disable (exact version), patch (allow patches), feature (allow feature updates), minor (allow minor updates), latestMajor (any). Use dotnet new globaljson to generate. Ensures consistent builds across environments.

interview.q: What is an SDK-style project and its advantages?
interview.a: SDK-style projects (introduced with .NET Core) use <Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">. Advantages: 1) Simplified csproj files, 2) Implicit file inclusion (**/*.cs), 3) Multi-targeting support, 4) PackageReference instead of packages.config, 5) Better MSBuild integration, 6) Conditional compilation symbols, 7) Improved NuGet support. Convert with dotnet new or migration tools.

interview.q: Explain the difference between TargetFramework and TargetFrameworks
interview.a: TargetFramework specifies single framework (net8.0). TargetFrameworks (plural) enables multi-targeting: <TargetFrameworks>net8.0;net7.0;netstandard2.1</TargetFrameworks>. Builds for all listed frameworks. Use for libraries supporting multiple .NET versions. Conditional compilation with #if NET8_0, #if NET7_0, etc. Each framework produces separate assembly.

interview.q: What are workloads in .NET?
interview.a: Workloads are optional SDK components for specific application types: MAUI, Blazor, ASP.NET, etc. Install with dotnet workload install maui. List with dotnet workload list. Update with dotnet workload update. Workloads reduce SDK size by installing only needed components. Managed via dotnet workload commands.

interview.q: How does dotnet CLI handle project references vs package references?
interview.a: ProjectReference: <ProjectReference Include="../Other/Other.csproj" /> for local projects, builds dependency, enables debugging. PackageReference: <PackageReference Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" /> for NuGet packages, restored from feeds. Project references are direct dependencies, package references are external. Use project references for closely related code, packages for external libraries.

interview.q: What is the purpose of Directory.Build.props and Directory.Build.targets?
interview.a: Directory.Build.props: Sets properties for all projects in directory and subdirectories, applied before project file. Directory.Build.targets: Defines targets, applied after project file. Used for: 1) Common properties (Nullable, LangVersion), 2) Shared package references, 3) Custom build tasks, 4) Company-wide standards. Enforce consistency across multiple projects.

interview.q: How do you manage different configurations (Debug, Release, etc.)?
interview.a: Configurations defined in csproj: <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Release'">. Use dotnet build --configuration Release. Define in Directory.Build.props for consistency. Custom configurations: <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Staging'">. Access in code with #if DEBUG, #if RELEASE. Configure different settings per configuration.

interview.q: What are the differences between framework-dependent and self-contained deployments?
interview.a: Framework-dependent: Requires .NET runtime installed on target machine, smaller deployment size. Self-contained: Includes .NET runtime, larger size, platform-specific. Specify with --self-contained flag in publish. Use framework-dependent for controlled environments, self-contained for maximum compatibility. Consider trimming to reduce size.

interview.q: How do you implement conditional compilation in .NET?
interview.a: Use #if directives with preprocessor symbols. Define in csproj: <DefineConstants>DEBUG;TRACE</DefineConstants>. Built-in symbols: DEBUG, RELEASE, NET8_0, NET7_0, etc. Use for: 1) Platform-specific code, 2) Debug-only features, 3) Feature toggles. Avoid excessive use; prefer dependency injection or configuration for runtime differences.

interview.q: What is MSBuild and how does it relate to dotnet CLI?
interview.a: MSBuild is Microsoft build engine, processes csproj files. dotnet CLI is wrapper around MSBuild with additional features. MSBuild properties control build: <PropertyGroup>, <ItemGroup>, <Target>. Custom tasks can be defined. Use dotnet msbuild to run MSBuild directly with advanced options. MSBuild is extensible with custom tasks and targets.

interview.q: How do you handle multiple environments (Development, Production)?
interview.a: 1) Use appsettings.{Environment}.json, 2) Set ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT variable, 3) Use IWebHostEnvironment, 4) Conditional configuration in Program.cs, 5) Different launchSettings.json profiles, 6) Environment-specific CI/CD pipelines. Access with Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT"). Development environment enables detailed error pages, etc.

interview.q: What are RID (Runtime Identifier) and when to use them?
interview.a: Runtime Identifier specifies target platform: win-x64, linux-x64, osx-arm64, etc. Use for: 1) Self-contained deployments, 2) Native dependencies, 3) Platform-specific optimizations. Specify in csproj: <RuntimeIdentifier>win-x64</RuntimeIdentifier> or during publish: dotnet publish -r win-x64. RID catalog defines available identifiers.

interview.q: How do you optimize build performance?
interview.a: 1) Enable parallel builds: dotnet build --max-cpu-count, 2) Use build servers: dotnet build-server, 3) Incremental builds, 4) Disable analyzers in CI, 5) Use reference assemblies, 6) Split large solutions, 7) Cache NuGet packages, 8) Use project references not binary references, 9) Enable ReadyToRun, 10) Use SourceLink for debugging efficiency.

interview.q: What is the difference between PackageReference and packages.config?
interview.a: PackageReference (SDK-style): References in csproj, transitive dependencies, better conflict resolution, supports Central Package Management. packages.config (legacy): Separate XML file, explicit all dependencies, used with .NET Framework. Migrate with dotnet migrate or Visual Studio. PackageReference is preferred for new projects.

interview.q: How do you implement code analysis and styling?
interview.a: 1) Use .editorconfig for code style, 2) Add analyzers: <PackageReference Include="StyleCop.Analyzers">, 3) Enable in csproj: <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>, 4) Use dotnet format, 5) Configure severity in .ruleset or editorconfig, 6) Use Roslyn analyzers, 7) Integrate with CI. Treat warnings as errors for consistency.

interview.q: What is Central Package Management?
interview.a: Feature to manage package versions centrally in Directory.Packages.props. Benefits: 1) Consistent versions across projects, 2) Single place to update, 3) Avoid version conflicts. Enable: <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>. Define versions in Directory.Packages.props, reference in csproj without version.

interview.q: How do you handle secrets in .NET projects?
interview.a: 1) User secrets: dotnet user-secrets set, accessed via IConfiguration, 2) Environment variables, 3) Azure Key Vault, 4) appsettings.json for non-secrets, 5) Secure storage for production. Use IConfiguration with multiple providers. Never commit secrets to source control. Use Secret Manager during development.

interview.q: What are the different project SDKs available?
interview.a: Microsoft.NET.Sdk: Console, library. Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web: ASP.NET Core. Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Worker: Worker service. Microsoft.NET.Sdk.BlazorWebAssembly: Blazor WASM. Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Razor: Razor class library. Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WindowsDesktop: WinForms/WPF. Specify in <Project Sdk="...">. Determines default properties and build behavior.

interview.q: How do you implement continuous integration with .NET?
interview.a: 1) Use GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps, Jenkins, 2) Restore: dotnet restore, 3) Build: dotnet build --configuration Release, 4) Test: dotnet test, 5) Publish: dotnet publish, 6) Package: dotnet pack, 7) Code analysis, 8) Coverage reporting. Use YAML pipelines, cache NuGet packages, parallel test execution, artifact publishing.

interview.q: What are source generators and how are they used?
interview.a: Source generators analyze code and generate additional source files during compilation. Use for: 1) Reducing boilerplate, 2) Performance optimizations, 3) Compile-time DI, 4) Serialization. Implement ISourceGenerator. Accessible via <PackageReference Include="MyGenerator"> with <OutputItemType>Analyzer</OutputItemType>. Examples: JSON serialization, logging, mapping.

csharp_nuget.title: NuGet & Package Management
csharp_nuget.summary: Publishing and consuming NuGet packages, versions, and private feeds.
csharp_nuget.details:
Publish libraries to NuGet with semantic versions. Use private feeds or Azure Artifacts for internal packages. Keep package references minimal and follow CVE checks.

NuGet Ecosystem:
1. Package Format: .nupkg (zip with metadata, DLLs, content)
2. NuGet.org: Public package repository
3. Private Feeds: Azure Artifacts, GitHub Packages, private NuGet Server
4. Package Management: Restore, update, conflict resolution

Package Lifecycle:
• Creation: dotnet pack, nuget pack
• Publishing: dotnet nuget push, nuget push
• Consumption: PackageReference in csproj
• Updates: Version ranges, transitive dependencies
• Security: Vulnerability scanning, deprecation
csharp_nuget.cheat:
// PACKAGE CREATION
// Basic package creation
dotnet pack --configuration Release

// With specific version
dotnet pack -c Release -p:PackageVersion=1.0.0

// Include symbols
dotnet pack -c Release --include-symbols --include-source

// PACKAGE PUBLISHING
// To NuGet.org
dotnet nuget push bin/Release/MyPackage.1.0.0.nupkg --api-key $NUGET_API_KEY --source https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json

// To private feed
dotnet nuget push bin/Release/*.nupkg --source https://pkgs.dev.azure.com/org/_packaging/feed/nuget/v3/index.json --api-key az

// With timeout
dotnet nuget push *.nupkg --source https://nuget.org --timeout 300

// PACKAGE CONSUMPTION
// Add package reference
dotnet add package Newtonsoft.Json --version 13.0.3

// Add with specific source
dotnet add package PrivatePackage --source https://privatefeed.example.com/nuget

// Update package
dotnet add package Newtonsoft.Json --version 13.0.4

dotnet update package Newtonsoft.Json  # Update to latest

// Remove package
dotnet remove package Newtonsoft.Json

// LIST AND SEARCH
// List installed packages
dotnet list package

dotnet list package --outdated  # Show outdated packages

dotnet list package --deprecated  # Show deprecated packages

dotnet list package --vulnerable  # Show vulnerable packages

// Search packages
dotnet search "json"

// RESTORE AND CLEAN
// Restore packages
dotnet restore

dotnet restore --no-cache  # Force restore

// Clear local cache
dotnet nuget locals all --clear

// PACKAGE METADATA IN CSPROJ
<PropertyGroup>
  <!-- Required metadata -->
  <PackageId>MyCompany.MyPackage</PackageId>
  <Version>1.0.0</Version>
  <Authors>Your Name</Authors>
  <Company>Your Company</Company>
  
  <!-- Optional metadata -->
  <Description>A useful package description</Description>
  <PackageTags>tag1;tag2;tag3</PackageTags>
  <PackageLicenseExpression>MIT</PackageLicenseExpression>
  <PackageProjectUrl>https://github.com/yourusername/mypackage</PackageProjectUrl>
  <RepositoryUrl>https://github.com/yourusername/mypackage.git</RepositoryUrl>
  <RepositoryType>git</RepositoryType>
  <Copyright>Copyright © 2024</Copyright>
  
  <!-- Icons -->
  <PackageIcon>icon.png</PackageIcon>
  <PackageIconUrl>https://example.com/icon.png</PackageIconUrl>
  
  <!-- Readme -->
  <PackageReadmeFile>README.md</PackageReadmeFile>
  
  <!-- Release notes -->
  <PackageReleaseNotes>Initial release with basic features.</PackageReleaseNotes>
  
  <!-- Dependencies -->
  <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
  
  <!-- Package settings -->
  <GeneratePackageOnBuild>true</GeneratePackageOnBuild>
  <IncludeSymbols>true</IncludeSymbols>
  <SymbolPackageFormat>snupkg</SymbolPackageFormat>
  <IncludeSource>true</IncludeSource>
</PropertyGroup>

<ItemGroup>
  <!-- Include files in package -->
  <None Include="icon.png" Pack="true" PackagePath="\" />
  <None Include="README.md" Pack="true" PackagePath="\" />
  <None Include="docs\**" Pack="true" PackagePath="docs\" />
  
  <!-- Exclude files -->
  <Compile Remove="**\*.test.cs" Pack="false" />
  
  <!-- Content files -->
  <Content Include="templates\**" Pack="true" PackagePath="contentFiles\any\any\templates\" />
</ItemGroup>

// VERSION RANGES
<PackageReference Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />  <!-- Exact -->
<PackageReference Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="[13.0.3]" /> <!-- Exact (bracket) -->
<PackageReference Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.*" /> <!-- Wildcard -->
<PackageReference Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3-*" /> <!-- Minimum, allow newer -->
<PackageReference Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="[13.0.3, 14.0.0)" /> <!-- Range -->

// PRIVATE FEED CONFIGURATION
// In NuGet.config
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<configuration>
  <packageSources>
    <clear />
    <add key="nuget.org" value="https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json" />
    <add key="PrivateFeed" value="https://pkgs.dev.azure.com/org/_packaging/feed/nuget/v3/index.json" />
  </packageSources>
  
  <packageSourceCredentials>
    <PrivateFeed>
      <add key="Username" value="PAT" />
      <add key="ClearTextPassword" value="$(PAT_TOKEN)" />
    </PrivateFeed>
  </packageSourceCredentials>
</configuration>

// Or in csproj
<PropertyGroup>
  <RestoreSources>
    https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json;
    https://privatefeed.example.com/nuget;
    $(RestoreSources)
  </RestoreSources>
</PropertyGroup>

// CENTRAL PACKAGE MANAGEMENT
// Directory.Packages.props
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>
  
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageVersion Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging" Version="8.0.0" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

// In csproj (no version specified)
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Newtonsoft.Json" />
</ItemGroup>

// GLOBAL TOOLS
// Install global tool
dotnet tool install -g dotnet-ef

// Install local tool (requires tool-manifest)
dotnet new tool-manifest
dotnet tool install dotnet-format

// Use local tool
dotnet tool restore  # Restore tools
dotnet dotnet-format  # Run tool

// List tools
dotnet tool list -g  # Global
dotnet tool list     # Local

// PACKAGE VALIDATION
// Check package for issues
dotnet nuget verify MyPackage.1.0.0.nupkg

// Sign package (requires certificate)
dotnet nuget sign MyPackage.1.0.0.nupkg --certificate-path cert.pfx --timestamper http://timestamp.digicert.com

// Workflow for creating and publishing a package
/*
# 1. Create package project
mkdir MyPackage
cd MyPackage
dotnet new classlib -n MyPackage

# 2. Add package metadata to csproj (as shown above)

# 3. Build and create package
dotnet build -c Release
dotnet pack -c Release --output ./nupkg

# 4. Test package locally
# Add local feed to NuGet.config
# Copy nupkg to local feed directory

# 5. Test consumption in another project
mkdir TestConsumer
cd TestConsumer
dotnet new console
# Add reference to local package
dotnet add package MyPackage --source ../MyPackage/nupkg

# 6. Publish to private feed (Azure DevOps example)
dotnet nuget push ./nupkg/*.nupkg \
  --source https://pkgs.dev.azure.com/org/_packaging/feed/nuget/v3/index.json \
  --api-key az

# 7. Publish to NuGet.org (requires API key)
dotnet nuget push ./nupkg/*.nupkg \
  --source https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json \
  --api-key $NUGET_API_KEY

# 8. Publish symbols separately (if using snupkg)
dotnet nuget push ./nupkg/*.snupkg \
  --source https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json \
  --api-key $NUGET_API_KEY
*/

// Versioning strategy example
/*
Semantic Versioning: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
- MAJOR: Incompatible API changes
- MINOR: New functionality (backwards compatible)
- PATCH: Bug fixes (backwards compatible)

Prerelease versions: 1.0.0-alpha.1, 1.0.0-beta.2, 1.0.0-rc.3

Examples:
1.0.0          # Initial release
1.0.1          # Bug fix
1.1.0          # New features
2.0.0          # Breaking changes
2.0.0-alpha.1  # First alpha
2.0.0-beta.1   # Beta release
2.0.0-rc.1     # Release candidate
2.0.0          # Final release

In csproj:
<Version>1.2.3</Version>                 # Stable
<Version>2.0.0-alpha.1</Version>         # Prerelease
<Version>2.0.0-beta.1+build.123</Version> # With build metadata
*/

// Managing dependencies and conflicts
/*
# Check for dependency conflicts
dotnet list package --include-transitive

# Force specific version (if conflict)
<PropertyGroup>
  <NewtonsoftJsonVersion>13.0.3</NewtonsoftJsonVersion>
</PropertyGroup>

<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="$(NewtonsoftJsonVersion)" />
</ItemGroup>

# Use PackageConflictPreferredPackages for resolution
<PropertyGroup>
  <PackageConflictPreferredPackages>Microsoft.Extensions.*;Newtonsoft.Json</PackageConflictPreferredPackages>
</PropertyGroup>

# Exclude transitive dependencies
<PackageReference Include="SomePackage" Version="1.0.0">
  <ExcludeAssets>runtime</ExcludeAssets>
  <PrivateAssets>all</PrivateAssets>
</PackageReference>
*/

// Security scanning and vulnerability management
/*
# Check for vulnerable packages
dotnet list package --vulnerable --include-transitive

# Check for deprecated packages
dotnet list package --deprecated

# Use dotnet-outdated tool to check for updates
dotnet tool install -g dotnet-outdated
dotnet outdated

# Use OSS Index or similar for security scanning
# Integrate with GitHub Dependabot or Azure DevOps security scanning

# Pin versions for security
dotnet add package Newtonsoft.Json --version 13.0.3

# Use Central Package Management for consistent versions
*/

mkdir MyTool
cd MyTool

dotnet nuget push ./nupkg/*.nupkg --source https://api.nuget.org/v3/index.json

# Users can install globally
dotnet tool install -g MyTool
mytool --help

interview.q: How to create and publish a NuGet package?
interview.a: 1) Create class library project, 2) Add package metadata in csproj (PackageId, Version, etc.), 3) Build with dotnet build, 4) Create package with dotnet pack, 5) Test locally with local feed, 6) Push with dotnet nuget push to feed (NuGet.org, private feed). Include symbols with --include-symbols. Sign packages for security. Follow semantic versioning.

interview.q: Why keep packages minimal in dependencies?
interview.a: Minimal dependencies: 1) Reduce transitive dependency chain, 2) Minimize security vulnerabilities, 3) Avoid version conflicts, 4) Reduce package size, 5) Faster restore/build times, 6) Easier maintenance. Only include essential dependencies. Use dependency analysis tools. Consider bundling for performance-critical libraries.

interview.q: Explain semantic versioning in NuGet packages
interview.a: SemVer: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (1.2.3). MAJOR: Breaking changes. MINOR: New features (backward compatible). PATCH: Bug fixes (backward compatible). Prerelease: 1.0.0-alpha.1, 1.0.0-beta.2. Build metadata: 1.0.0+build.123. NuGet supports version ranges: [1.0.0] exact, (1.0.0,) greater than, (,2.0.0) less than. Use consistent versioning for predictable updates.

interview.q: What are private feeds and how to set them up?
interview.a: Private feeds host internal packages. Options: 1) Azure Artifacts, 2) GitHub Packages, 3) NuGet.Server, 4) ProGet, 5) Local directory. Configure in NuGet.config with <packageSources>. Add authentication for secure feeds. Use source mapping to control which packages come from which feed. Cache packages for performance.

interview.q: How do you handle package version conflicts?
interview.a: 1) Use Central Package Management for consistent versions, 2) Specify exact versions, 3) Use PackageConflictPreferredPackages, 4) Analyze with dotnet list package --include-transitive, 5) Use binding redirects (legacy), 6) Update all packages to compatible versions, 7) Consider consolidating dependencies, 8) Use dependency exclusion where appropriate.

interview.q: What is Central Package Management and its benefits?
interview.a: Central Package Management (Directory.Packages.props) centralizes package versions. Benefits: 1) Single place for version management, 2) Consistent versions across projects, 3) Easy updates, 4) Reduced conflicts, 5) Better visibility. Enable with <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>. Define versions in Directory.Packages.props.

interview.q: How do you secure NuGet packages?
interview.a: 1) Sign packages with certificates, 2) Use private feeds with authentication, 3) Scan for vulnerabilities (dotnet list package --vulnerable), 4) Keep dependencies updated, 5) Use lock files for reproducible builds, 6) Validate packages before use, 7) Implement supply chain security, 8) Use package source mapping to control sources.

interview.q: What are the differences between packages.config and PackageReference?
interview.a: packages.config: Legacy, separate XML file, explicit all dependencies, used with .NET Framework. PackageReference: In csproj, transitive dependencies, better conflict resolution, supports Central Package Management. PackageReference is preferred. Migrate with dotnet migrate or Visual Studio. PackageReference enables faster restores and cleaner projects.

interview.q: How do you create multi-targeting packages?
interview.a: Use <TargetFrameworks> (plural) with multiple TFM: net8.0;net7.0;netstandard2.0. Use conditional compilation: #if NET8_0, #if NETSTANDARD2_0. Framework-specific references with Condition attribute. Test on all targets. Consider API compatibility across frameworks. Use lowest common denominator for maximum compatibility.

interview.q: What are global tools and how to create them?
interview.a: Global tools are console applications distributed as NuGet packages. Create: 1) Console app, 2) Add <PackAsTool>true</PackAsTool>, 3) Set <ToolCommandName>, 4) dotnet pack, 5) dotnet tool install -g. Users run with tool command name. Local tools use tool manifest. Useful for CLI utilities, code generators, development tools.

interview.q: How do you manage transitive dependencies?
interview.a: Transitive dependencies are automatically included. Manage by: 1) Review with dotnet list package --include-transitive, 2) Use Central Package Management, 3) Pin versions for critical dependencies, 4) Exclude assets where appropriate, 5) Use dependency graphs to understand relationships, 6) Update parent packages to get newer transitive versions.

interview.q: What is package source mapping and why use it?
interview.a: Package source mapping restricts packages to specific feeds for security. Configure in NuGet.config with <packageSourceMapping>. Prevents dependency confusion attacks. Example: internal packages only from private feed, public packages from nuget.org. Ensures packages come from trusted sources. Requires NuGet 6.0+.

interview.q: How do you handle package restoration in CI/CD?
interview.a: 1) Cache NuGet packages between builds, 2) Use locked dependencies (lock files), 3) Parallel restoration, 4) Private feed authentication, 5) Fallback feeds for reliability, 6) Restore with --no-cache for clean builds, 7) Validate package signatures, 8) Check for vulnerabilities during restore. Optimize for speed and security.

interview.q: What are symbol packages and debugging symbols?
interview.a: Symbol packages (.snupkg) contain debugging symbols (PDB files). Enable with <IncludeSymbols>true</IncludeSymbols>. Allow debugging into package source. SourceLink embeds source control information. Configure with <EmbedUntrackedSources>true</EmbedUntrackedSources>. Publish symbols to symbol server. Users can debug package code in Visual Studio.

interview.q: How do you deprecate or unlist packages?
interview.a: Deprecate: Mark package as deprecated with message (security, obsolete). Unlist: Remove from search but keep downloadable. Delete: Permanent removal (limited scenarios). Use dotnet nuget delete or NuGet.org interface. Consider migration path for users. Update package metadata with deprecation notice. Use vulnerability reporting for security issues.

interview.q: What are content files in NuGet packages?
interview.a: Content files copied to consuming project: templates, config files, scripts. Include with <Content Include="..." Pack="true" PackagePath="contentFiles/...">. Control with contentFiles element in nuspec. Can be conditionally included based on target framework. Useful for scaffolding, default configurations, sample files.

interview.q: How do you implement automatic package updates?
interview.a: 1) Use Dependabot (GitHub), 2) Azure DevOps security scanning, 3) Renovate bot, 4) dotnet-outdated tool, 5) CI pipeline with automatic PR creation, 6) Version range wildcards (caret ^, tilde ~), 7) Scheduled updates. Balance automation with testing requirements. Major updates may require manual review.

interview.q: What are the best practices for package metadata?
interview.a: 1) Meaningful PackageId (Company.Library), 2) Clear Description, 3) Proper Tags for discoverability, 4) License information (SPDX expression), 5) Repository URL, 6) Authors and Company, 7) Icon for visual identification, 8) Readme file, 9) Release notes, 10) Copyright. Complete metadata improves usability and trust.

interview.q: How do you test packages before publishing?
interview.a: 1) Local feed testing, 2) Consumer test projects, 3) Integration tests, 4) Compatibility testing on different frameworks, 5) Performance testing, 6) Security scanning, 7) API compatibility checks, 8) Documentation verification. Use prerelease versions for testing. Automate testing in CI pipeline before publishing.

interview.q: What is the difference between nupkg and snupkg?
interview.a: nupkg: Main package containing DLLs, metadata, content files. snupkg: Symbol package containing PDB files for debugging. Created with <SymbolPackageFormat>snupkg</SymbolPackageFormat>. Published separately to symbol server. Users with Visual Studio can step into package code. snupkg is optional but recommended for libraries.

csharp_interop.title: Interop & P/Invoke
csharp_interop.summary: Call native code using P/Invoke, COM interop and use of System.Runtime.InteropServices.
csharp_interop.details:
Use P/Invoke for calling native libraries carefully: manage marshaling, calling conventions and ownership. For .NET/Java interop use gRPC or REST when possible.

Interop Types:
1. Platform Invoke (P/Invoke): Call native DLLs from managed code
2. COM Interop: Interoperate with COM components
3. C++/CLI: Mixed-mode assemblies for deep integration
4. Native AOT: Compile directly to native code
5. JavaScript Interop: For Blazor WebAssembly

Key Concepts:
• Marshaling: Converting between managed and unmanaged types
• Calling Conventions: cdecl, stdcall, thiscall, fastcall
• Memory Management: Who allocates/frees memory
• Platform Detection: Runtime checks for OS-specific code
• SafeHandles: Safe wrapper for native handles

interview.q: What are the main considerations when using P/Invoke?
interview.a: 1) Marshaling: Convert between managed/unmanaged types correctly, 2) Memory Management: Who allocates/frees memory, 3) Platform Differences: 32/64-bit, OS-specific APIs, 4) Calling Conventions: cdecl vs stdcall, 5) String Encoding: ANSI vs Unicode, 6) Error Handling: GetLastError and SetLastError, 7) Thread Safety: Reentrancy issues, 8) Performance: Marshaling overhead, 9) Security: Validate inputs, 10) Versioning: API changes between OS versions.

interview.q: When should you avoid interop and what are the alternatives?
interview.a: Avoid interop when: 1) Managed alternatives exist (System.IO vs Win32 file APIs), 2) Cross-platform support needed, 3) Security critical code, 4) Performance not critical. Alternatives: 1) Pure managed libraries, 2) Web APIs/REST for cross-language, 3) gRPC for high-performance RPC, 4) Message queues, 5) Database as integration point, 6) File-based exchange. Interop adds complexity and platform dependencies.

interview.q: Explain marshaling in .NET interop
interview.a: Marshaling converts data between managed (.NET) and unmanaged (native) memory. Types: 1) Blittable: Same representation (int, float), 2) Non-blittable: Require conversion (string, bool). Use MarshalAs attribute for control. String marshaling: LPStr (ANSI), LPWStr (Unicode), BStr (COM). Array marshaling: specify size. Custom marshaling with ICustomMarshaler. Marshaling has performance cost.

interview.q: What is the difference between DllImport and LibraryImport?
interview.a: DllImport: Traditional attribute, runtime reflection-based, more error-prone. LibraryImport (C# 9+): Source generator, compile-time validation, better performance, supports default marshaling. LibraryImport generates optimized code, enables trimming, provides better diagnostics. Use LibraryImport for new code, DllImport for compatibility. Both require System.Runtime.InteropServices.

interview.q: How do you handle platform-specific code in .NET?
interview.a: 1) Runtime detection: RuntimeInformation.IsOSPlatform(), 2) Conditional compilation: #if WINDOWS, #if LINUX, 3) Separate implementations per platform, 4) Dependency injection with platform-specific implementations, 5) Use Microsoft.Extensions.PlatformAbstractions. Define constants in csproj: <DefineConstants>$(DefineConstants);WINDOWS</DefineConstants>. Load platform-specific native libraries dynamically.

interview.q: What are SafeHandles and why use them?
interview.a: SafeHandle wraps native handles ensuring proper cleanup. Benefits: 1) Automatic cleanup via Dispose/finalizer, 2) Prevention of handle recycling attacks, 3) Thread-safe reference counting, 4) Integration with using statements. Derive from SafeHandleZeroOrMinusOneIsInvalid. Override ReleaseHandle() for cleanup. Use instead of IntPtr for handles.

interview.q: How do you debug P/Invoke issues?
interview.a: 1) Check DllImport attributes (CharSet, CallingConvention), 2) Use SetLastError=true and Marshal.GetLastWin32Error(), 3) Verify platform (32/64-bit), 4) Check memory ownership (who frees), 5) Use DebuggerStepThrough, 6) Log marshaling, 7) Use Platform Invocation Assistant (PInvoke.net), 8) Test with small examples, 9) Check DLL search path, 10) Use Dependency Walker for DLL dependencies.

interview.q: Explain COM interop in .NET
interview.a: COM interop bridges .NET and COM components. Two directions: 1) COM Callable Wrapper (CCW): .NET object exposed to COM, 2) Runtime Callable Wrapper (RCW): COM object used in .NET. Use [ComVisible(true)] attribute. Marshal COM objects with Marshal.ReleaseComObject(). Handle COM apartments (STA/MTA). Use primary interop assemblies (PIAs) for Office automation.

interview.q: What are the performance implications of interop?
interview.a: Overhead includes: 1) Marshaling data (especially strings/arrays), 2) Transition between managed/unmanaged (approx 30-100 cycles), 3) Pinning memory for callbacks, 4) Exception translation. Optimize by: 1) Minimize cross-boundary calls, 2) Use blittable types, 3) Batch operations, 4) Use unsafe code for large data, 5) Consider C++/CLI for performance-critical interop.

interview.q: How do you handle 32-bit vs 64-bit interop?
interview.a: 1) Use IntPtr for pointers (not int), 2) Check IntPtr.Size for pointer size, 3) Use [DllImport] without EntryPoint for name mangling, 4) Load different DLLs based on platform, 5) Handle different structure layouts (packing), 6) Use AnyCPU or platform-specific builds, 7) Be careful with long vs int on 64-bit. Test on both architectures.

interview.q: What is the role of System.Runtime.InteropServices.Marshal class?
interview.a: Marshal class provides methods for: 1) Allocating/freeing unmanaged memory (AllocHGlobal, FreeHGlobal), 2) Copying between managed/unmanaged (Copy, StructureToPtr), 3) String conversion (StringToHGlobalAnsi), 4) COM object management (ReleaseComObject), 5) Getting function pointers (GetFunctionPointerForDelegate), 6) Size calculation (SizeOf). Essential for advanced interop scenarios.

interview.q: How do you implement callbacks from native code?
interview.a: 1) Define delegate with matching signature, 2) Use [UnmanagedFunctionPointer] attribute with CallingConvention, 3) Pass delegate to native function, 4) Keep delegate alive (GC won't collect), 5) Handle exceptions in callback (they can't cross native boundary). Use GCHandle.Alloc() to pin delegate. Consider thread safety in callbacks.

interview.q: What are the security considerations for interop?
interview.a: 1) Validate all inputs from native code, 2) Use SafeHandles to prevent handle attacks, 3) Be careful with buffer overflows in native code, 4) Limit exposed surface area, 5) Run native code with minimal privileges, 6) Audit native dependencies, 7) Use code signing for native DLLs, 8) Consider AppContainer sandboxing on Windows, 9) Use secure string marshaling.

interview.q: How do you handle exceptions in interop scenarios?
interview.a: 1) Native exceptions don't cross boundary (SEHException), 2) Check return values and GetLastError(), 3) Use try-catch for managed exceptions, 4) Translate native errors to .NET exceptions, 5) Handle AccessViolationException for memory issues, 6) Use [HandleProcessCorruptedStateExceptions] for critical exceptions. Design error handling contract between managed/unmanaged.

interview.q: What is C++/CLI and when to use it?
interview.a: C++/CLI is Microsoft's language for mixed-mode assemblies combining managed/unmanaged code. Use when: 1) Need deep integration with C++ libraries, 2) Performance-critical interop, 3) Complex native APIs, 4) Wrapping legacy C++ code. Creates bridges without marshaling overhead. Results in larger assemblies. Use #pragma managed/unmanaged to mix code.

interview.q: How do you test interop code?
interview.a: 1) Unit test managed wrapper logic, 2) Integration tests with actual native DLLs, 3) Test on all target platforms, 4) Test 32/64-bit separately, 5) Mock native calls for unit tests, 6) Use TestServer for COM automation tests, 7) Test error conditions (invalid inputs), 8) Performance test cross-boundary calls, 9) Use continuous integration with multiple OS images.

interview.q: What are the best practices for interop code?
interview.a: 1) Isolate interop in separate layer, 2) Use SafeHandles for resources, 3) Validate all parameters, 4) Provide managed-friendly API, 5) Document platform requirements, 6) Use conditional compilation for platform-specific code, 7) Implement IDisposable for cleanup, 8) Use LibraryImport when possible, 9) Test thoroughly on all platforms, 10) Keep interop surface minimal.

interview.q: How do you handle versioning of native dependencies?
interview.a: 1) Load DLLs dynamically with LoadLibrary, 2) Check function existence with GetProcAddress, 3) Provide fallback implementations, 4) Use delay loading, 5) Bundle specific DLL versions, 6) Use side-by-side assemblies, 7) Document version requirements, 8) Use feature detection not version detection, 9) Consider API wrapping with version abstraction layer.

interview.q: What is Blazor JavaScript interop?
interview.a: Blazor's mechanism to call JavaScript from C# and vice versa. Use IJSRuntime.InvokeAsync for JavaScript calls. Use [JSInvokable] to expose C# methods to JavaScript. Handles JSON serialization automatically. Use for: DOM manipulation, JavaScript libraries, browser APIs. Consider performance for frequent calls. Use JavaScript isolation for component-specific code.

interview.q: How do you optimize interop performance?
interview.a: 1) Minimize cross-boundary calls (batch operations), 2) Use blittable types when possible, 3) Prefer LibraryImport over DllImport, 4) Use unsafe code for large data blocks, 5) Cache delegate instances for callbacks, 6) Use fixed buffers with stackalloc, 7) Consider C++/CLI for critical paths, 8) Profile and measure overhead, 9) Use native AOT for startup performance.

csharp_testing.title: Testing & xUnit/NUnit
csharp_testing.summary: xUnit/NUnit, mocking (Moq), integration tests, test fixtures and CI integration.
csharp_testing.details:
Write unit tests with xUnit or NUnit, use Moq for mocks, and isolate external dependencies. Use TestServer for ASP.NET integration tests and run tests in CI with coverage reports.

Testing Pyramid:
1. Unit Tests: Test individual components in isolation
2. Integration Tests: Test component interactions
3. End-to-End Tests: Test complete workflows

Testing Frameworks:
• xUnit: Modern, extensible, community-driven
• NUnit: Feature-rich, mature
• MSTest: Microsoft's framework
• bUnit: Blazor component testing

Mocking Libraries:
• Moq: Most popular mocking library
• NSubstitute: Clean syntax
• FakeItEasy: Easy to use
• Microsoft Fakes: Isolates code for testing

interview.q: How do you test async methods in xUnit/NUnit?
interview.a: Make test method async and return Task/Task<T>. Use await to call async methods. xUnit/NUnit support async tests natively. For exceptions: use Assert.ThrowsAsync<T>(). Example: public async Task TestAsync() { await method(); }. Ensure test cleans up async operations. Use ConfigureAwait(false) in tests to avoid deadlocks.

interview.q: What is TestServer and when to use it?
interview.a: TestServer (WebApplicationFactory<T>) creates in-memory test server for ASP.NET Core integration tests. Use for: 1) Testing controllers/endpoints without real HTTP, 2) Integration tests with full middleware pipeline, 3) Testing authentication/authorization, 4) Database integration tests. Provides HttpClient to call endpoints. Faster than real HTTP, includes dependency injection container.

interview.q: Explain the Arrange-Act-Assert pattern
interview.a: AAA is test structure pattern: Arrange: Setup test data, mocks, objects. Act: Execute method being tested. Assert: Verify results match expectations. Improves test readability. Each test should test one thing. Keep tests simple and focused. Example: Arrange calculator, Act call Add(2,3), Assert result is 5.

interview.q: What are the differences between xUnit, NUnit, and MSTest?
interview.a: xUnit: Modern, extensible, no [SetUp]/[TearDown] (use constructor/dispose), [Fact]/[Theory], community-driven. NUnit: Feature-rich, mature, [SetUp]/[TearDown], [Test]/[TestCase], parameterized tests. MSTest: Microsoft's, integrated with Visual Studio, [TestInitialize]/[TestCleanup], good for enterprise. Choose xUnit for new projects, NUnit for feature needs, MSTest for VS integration.

interview.q: How do you mock dependencies in unit tests?
interview.a: Use mocking libraries: Moq (popular), NSubstitute (clean syntax), FakeItEasy. Steps: 1) Create Mock<T>, 2) Setup methods/properties (Setup, Returns), 3) Verify calls (Verify, Times). Mock external dependencies: databases, HTTP clients, file systems. Use dependency injection to inject mocks. Avoid mocking value objects, complex objects, or test subjects.

interview.q: What are test fixtures and when to use them?
interview.a: Test fixtures provide shared context across multiple tests. Use for expensive setup: database, web server, configuration. In xUnit: IClassFixture<T> for class-level, ICollectionFixture<T> for collection-level. Implement IDisposable for cleanup. Share state between tests in same class/collection. Avoid test interdependence - each test should be independent.

interview.q: How do you handle database testing?
interview.a: Options: 1) In-memory database (EF Core InMemory), 2) SQLite in-memory, 3) Test container with real database, 4) Local database instance. Use transactions to isolate tests. Seed test data in fixture. Clean up after tests. Consider integration tests vs unit tests (mock repository). Use database migration in tests.

interview.q: What is the difference between unit, integration, and end-to-end tests?
interview.a: Unit tests: Test single component in isolation, fast, mock dependencies. Integration tests: Test component interactions (database, APIs), slower, use real dependencies. End-to-end tests: Test complete user workflows, slowest, simulate real user. Testing pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration, least E2E. Each catches different bugs.

interview.q: How do you test private methods?
interview.a: Generally don't test private methods directly - test via public API. If needed: 1) Make method internal and use InternalsVisibleTo, 2) Reflection (not recommended), 3) Extract to separate class, 4) Use private accessor (legacy). Private methods are implementation details; public behavior matters. If complex, consider refactoring.

interview.q: What are parameterized tests and how to implement them?
interview.a: Parameterized tests run same test logic with different inputs. In xUnit: [Theory] with [InlineData], [MemberData], [ClassData]. In NUnit: [TestCase]. Provide test data as parameters. Example: [Theory] [InlineData(1,2,3)] void AddTest(int a, int b, int expected). Tests multiple scenarios without code duplication. Use for boundary values, edge cases.

interview.q: How do you measure code coverage?
interview.a: Use tools: 1) coverlet (cross-platform), 2) Visual Studio coverage, 3) dotCover, 4) OpenCover. Run: dotnet test --collect:"XPlat Code Coverage". Generate reports with ReportGenerator. Aim for 70-80% coverage (not 100%). Focus on critical paths. Coverage shows what's tested, not test quality. Integrate with CI/CD.

interview.q: What are the best practices for test naming?
interview.a: Use descriptive names: MethodName_Scenario_ExpectedBehavior. Examples: Add_TwoPositiveNumbers_ReturnsSum, ProcessOrder_InvalidOrder_ThrowsException, GetUser_NonExistentId_ReturnsNull. Follow consistent convention. Names should document test purpose. Avoid generic names like Test1. Makes test failures self-documenting.

interview.q: How do you test ASP.NET Core controllers?
interview.a: 1) Unit tests: Mock dependencies, test action logic, 2) Integration tests: Use TestServer, test full pipeline, 3) Test ModelState validation, 4) Test authentication/authorization attributes, 5) Test response types/status codes. Use ActionResult<T> for type-safe testing. Test filters, middleware. Consider API contract testing.

interview.q: What is dependency injection in testing?
interview.a: Use DI to inject mocks/fakes into test subjects. Configure test DI container differently from production. In ASP.NET Core tests: Use WebApplicationFactory with ConfigureTestServices. Replace real services with test doubles. Test different configurations. DI makes testing easier by enabling loose coupling and test substitution.

interview.q: How do you test exceptions?
interview.a: Use Assert.Throws<T>(action) or Assert.ThrowsAsync<T>(asyncAction). Verify exception message, properties. Test that exception is thrown for invalid inputs. Test custom exception types. Ensure exception is not caught and swallowed. Test exception in async code with ThrowsAsync. Verify exception contains expected data.

interview.q: What are test doubles (mocks, stubs, fakes)?
interview.a: Test doubles replace real dependencies: Mock: Verifies interactions (expects calls). Stub: Provides canned responses, doesn't verify. Fake: Working implementation for testing (in-memory database). Spy: Records interactions for verification. Dummy: Placeholder, never used. Choose based on test needs. Moq can create mocks/stubs.

interview.q: How do you handle flaky tests?
interview.a: Flaky tests fail intermittently. Causes: timing issues, shared state, external dependencies. Fix by: 1) Make tests independent, 2) Use proper synchronization, 3) Mock time-dependent code, 4) Retry transient failures, 5) Isolate external dependencies, 6) Use test timeouts, 7) Investigate root cause. Flaky tests reduce trust in test suite.

interview.q: What is behavior-driven development (BDD) in .NET?
interview.a: BDD focuses on system behavior from user perspective. Frameworks: SpecFlow (Gherkin syntax), xBehave, NSpec. Write tests in Given-When-Then format. Business-readable specifications. Integrates with testing frameworks. Use for acceptance criteria, collaboration with non-technical stakeholders. Generates living documentation.

interview.q: How do you test concurrent code?
interview.a: Test multithreaded code by: 1) Testing single-threaded logic, 2) Using ThreadPool or Task, 3) Testing race conditions, 4) Using synchronization primitives in tests, 5) Stress testing with multiple threads, 6) Testing cancellation, 7) Using async testing patterns. Hard to test reliably - focus on thread-safe design.

interview.q: What are the common testing anti-patterns?
interview.a: 1) Testing implementation details, 2) Over-mocking, 3) Fragile tests (break on refactoring), 4) Slow tests, 5) Test interdependence, 6) Not testing error cases, 7) Ignoring test failures, 8) Magic strings/numbers, 9) Testing private methods, 10) No assertions (test passes always). Write maintainable, focused tests.

csharp_security.title: Security & Best Practices
csharp_security.summary: Protect secrets, validate inputs, use HTTPS, and manage dependencies.
csharp_security.details:
Use secure configuration (Azure Key Vault, AWS Secret Manager), enforce HTTPS, validate inputs server-side, use built-in cryptography APIs and sanitize data. Keep dependencies updated and run static security scanners.

Security Pillars:
1. Confidentiality: Protect data from unauthorized access
2. Integrity: Ensure data is not tampered with
3. Availability: Ensure systems are accessible
4. Authentication: Verify identity
5. Authorization: Control access to resources

Common Threats:
• Injection Attacks: SQL, OS, LDAP injection
• Cross-Site Scripting (XSS): Injecting malicious scripts
• Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF): Unauthorized commands
• Insecure Deserialization: Remote code execution
• Security Misconfiguration: Default settings, exposed ports

interview.q: How to store secrets securely in .NET applications?
interview.a: 1) Development: Use User Secrets (dotnet user-secrets), 2) Production: Use Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, 3) Environment variables (for containers), 4) Secure configuration providers, 5) Never store secrets in source code or appsettings.json, 6) Use Managed Identities in Azure, 7) Rotate secrets regularly, 8) Audit secret access. Use IConfiguration with multiple providers for different environments.

interview.q: How to protect APIs from common attacks?
interview.a: 1) Use HTTPS/TLS encryption, 2) Implement authentication (JWT, OAuth 2.0), 3) Use authorization (roles, policies), 4) Validate all inputs server-side, 5) Implement rate limiting, 6) Use API keys for programmatic access, 7) Add security headers (CSP, HSTS), 8) Log security events, 9) Use API gateways for additional protection, 10) Regular security testing and penetration testing.

interview.q: Explain the principle of least privilege
interview.a: Grant only the minimum permissions necessary for a user/process to perform its function. In .NET: 1) Use specific roles not admin, 2) Run services with minimal permissions, 3) Limit database permissions (read vs write), 4) Use file system ACLs, 5) Container isolation, 6) Network segmentation. Reduces attack surface and limits damage from compromised accounts.

interview.q: How do you prevent SQL injection in .NET?
interview.a: 1) Always use parameterized queries (Entity Framework, Dapper), 2) Never concatenate SQL strings, 3) Use ORM's LINQ queries, 4) Validate and sanitize inputs, 5) Use stored procedures with parameters, 6) Apply principle of least privilege to database accounts, 7) Use EF Core's FromSqlRaw/FromSqlInterpolated safely. Input validation alone is not sufficient - always parameterize.

interview.q: What is Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) and how to prevent it?
interview.a: XSS injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by others. Prevention: 1) HTML encode all user inputs (HttpUtility.HtmlEncode), 2) Use anti-XSS libraries, 3) Implement Content Security Policy (CSP), 4) Validate and sanitize inputs, 5) Use HttpOnly cookies, 6) Encode data in the correct context (HTML, JavaScript, CSS, URL). Razor automatically HTML encodes by default.

interview.q: How do you implement secure authentication?
interview.a: 1) Use proven libraries (ASP.NET Core Identity), 2) Hash passwords with strong algorithms (PBKDF2, Argon2, bcrypt), 3) Use salts, 4) Implement multi-factor authentication, 5) Secure password reset flows, 6) Use HTTPS, 7) Implement account lockout after failed attempts, 8) Use secure session management, 9) Regularly rotate secrets, 10) Monitor for suspicious activity.

interview.q: What is CSRF and how to prevent it?
interview.a: Cross-Site Request Forgery tricks users into executing unwanted actions. Prevention: 1) Use anti-forgery tokens (ASP.NET Core's [ValidateAntiForgeryToken]), 2) SameSite cookies, 3) Check referrer headers, 4) Use custom headers for AJAX, 5) Require re-authentication for sensitive operations. ASP.NET Core has built-in anti-forgery with FormTagHelper and AJAX support.

interview.q: How do you secure file uploads?
interview.a: 1) Validate file type (extension AND content), 2) Limit file size, 3) Scan for malware, 4) Sanitize filenames, 5) Store outside web root, 6) Use random filenames, 7) Set proper permissions, 8) Use content-disposition: attachment for downloads, 9) Implement virus scanning, 10) Restrict allowed file types to minimum needed.

interview.q: What are security headers and which ones to use?
interview.a: HTTP headers that enhance security: 1) Content-Security-Policy (CSP): controls resources, 2) X-Frame-Options: prevents clickjacking, 3) X-Content-Type-Options: prevents MIME sniffing, 4) X-XSS-Protection: legacy XSS protection, 5) Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS): enforces HTTPS, 6) Referrer-Policy: controls referrer info. Implement via middleware or web.config.

interview.q: How do you handle sensitive data in logs?
interview.a: 1) Never log passwords, tokens, PII, 2) Use structured logging with masking, 3) Configure log levels appropriately, 4) Secure log storage, 5) Encrypt sensitive logs, 6) Implement log retention policies, 7) Use log redaction libraries, 8) Audit log access, 9) Be careful with exception messages. Use Serilog enrichers or custom middleware for redaction.

interview.q: What is the Data Protection API in ASP.NET Core?
interview.a: DPAPI provides cryptographic APIs for data protection. Features: 1) Key management and rotation, 2) Data encryption at rest, 3) Purpose strings for isolation, 4) Support for distributed scenarios. Use for: protecting cookies, tokens, sensitive data. Configure with PersistKeysToFileSystem, PersistKeysToAzureBlobStorage. Essential for farm deployments.

interview.q: How do you secure a .NET application in production?
interview.a: 1) Use HTTPS everywhere, 2) Keep .NET runtime and packages updated, 3) Remove development middleware, 4) Use production configuration, 5) Implement monitoring and alerting, 6) Regular security scanning, 7) Use WAF (Web Application Firewall), 8) Network segmentation, 9) Regular backups, 10) Incident response plan. Follow OWASP Top 10 guidelines.

interview.q: What is dependency vulnerability scanning?
interview.a: Scanning third-party packages for known vulnerabilities. Tools: 1) dotnet list package --vulnerable, 2) GitHub Dependabot, 3) OWASP Dependency Check, 4) NuGet security audit, 5) SonarQube. Integrate into CI/CD pipeline. Regularly update packages. Use lock files for reproducible builds. Review dependencies before adding.

interview.q: How do you implement rate limiting?
interview.a: Limit requests to prevent abuse/DoS. ASP.NET Core 7+: built-in rate limiting middleware. Configure: Fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, concurrent. Apply per IP, user, or endpoint. Return 429 Too Many Requests. Use caching for expensive operations. Consider distributed rate limiting for scaled applications. Log rate limit hits.

interview.q: What is secure coding practice for cryptography?
interview.a: 1) Never implement your own crypto algorithms, 2) Use System.Security.Cryptography, 3) Use strong algorithms (AES-256, SHA-256, RSA-2048+), 4) Generate cryptographically random numbers (RandomNumberGenerator), 5) Use proper key management, 6) Store keys securely (not in code), 7) Use constant-time comparisons, 8) Keep crypto libraries updated, 9) Follow algorithm recommendations from authorities.

interview.q: How do you secure communication between microservices?
interview.a: 1) Mutual TLS (mTLS) for authentication and encryption, 2) API gateways with authentication, 3) Service meshes (Istio, Linkerd), 4) JWT tokens with validation, 5) Network policies for segmentation, 6) Encrypt sensitive data at rest, 7) Use secure service discovery, 8) Implement zero-trust network, 9) Regular certificate rotation, 10) Audit inter-service communication.

interview.q: What are the OWASP Top 10 and how to address them?
interview.a: OWASP Top 10 web app security risks: 1) Broken Access Control: implement proper authorization, 2) Cryptographic Failures: use TLS, hash passwords, 3) Injection: parameterize queries, 4) Insecure Design: threat modeling, secure by design, 5) Security Misconfiguration: harden systems, 6) Vulnerable Components: update dependencies, 7) Authentication Failures: MFA, strong passwords, 8) Software/Data Integrity: code signing, CI/CD security, 9) Security Logging/Monitoring: audit logs, 10) SSRF: validate URLs, network segmentation.

interview.q: How do you handle security in CI/CD pipelines?
interview.a: 1) Scan code for secrets before commit, 2) Dependency vulnerability scanning, 3) Static application security testing (SAST), 4) Dynamic application security testing (DAST), 5) Container image scanning, 6) Infrastructure as code security, 7) Secure artifact storage, 8) Automated security tests, 9) Environment-specific configuration, 10) Security approval gates. Shift security left in development process.

interview.q: What is defense in depth?
interview.a: Multiple layers of security controls so if one fails, others provide protection. Layers: 1) Network (firewalls, segmentation), 2) Host (OS hardening, antivirus), 3) Application (input validation, authentication), 4) Data (encryption, backups), 5) Physical security, 6) Policies and procedures. In .NET: validate at multiple levels (client, server, database).

interview.q: How do you conduct security testing for .NET apps?
interview.a: 1) Static analysis (SonarQube, Roslyn analyzers), 2) Dynamic analysis (penetration testing), 3) Dependency scanning, 4) Security unit tests, 5) Fuzz testing, 6) Manual code review, 7) Threat modeling, 8) Security scanning tools (Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP), 9) Red team exercises, 10) Compliance scanning (PCI DSS, HIPAA). Integrate into development lifecycle.

csharp_performance.title: Performance & Profiling
csharp_performance.summary: Comprehensive performance optimization strategies for .NET applications including benchmarking, profiling, diagnostics, and memory management techniques. Covers tools, methodologies, and code patterns to identify and resolve performance bottlenecks in production and development environments.
csharp_performance.details:
**Core Performance Concepts:**
1. **Benchmarking & Profiling Tools**
   - **BenchmarkDotNet**: Industry-standard for microbenchmarking with statistical analysis, memory diagnostics, and cross-platform support
   - **Production Tools**: dotnet-trace (runtime traces), dotnet-counters (real-time monitoring), dotnet-dump (crash analysis), PerfView (system-wide analysis)
   - **Monitoring**: Application Insights, OpenTelemetry, custom metrics with System.Diagnostics.Metrics

2. **Memory Management & GC Optimization**
   - **GC Generations**: Understanding Gen0, Gen1, Gen2 collections and Large Object Heap (LOH)
   - **Allocation Reduction**: Avoid boxing, use structs wisely, implement object pooling
   - **Span<T> and Memory<T>**: Zero-allocation operations on contiguous memory
   - **ArrayPool<T>**: Reuse arrays to reduce GC pressure
   - **Weak References**: Prevent memory leaks in caching scenarios

3. **CPU & Concurrency Optimization**
   - **SIMD Operations**: Vector<T> for data-parallel processing
   - **Threading Models**: Task Parallel Library, async/await best practices
   - **Locking Strategies**: ReaderWriterLockSlim, SpinLock, Concurrent collections
   - **CPU Cache Awareness**: Data locality and cache-friendly structures

4. **I/O Performance**
   - **Async I/O**: Proper use of FileStream, HttpClient, and database operations
   - **Buffering Strategies**: Optimal buffer sizes for network and file operations
   - **Pipelining**: System.IO.Pipelines for high-performance streaming

5. **JIT & AOT Compilation**
   - **Tiered Compilation**: Adaptive optimization based on usage
   - **ReadyToRun (R2R)**: Ahead-of-time compilation for faster startup
   - **Profile-Guided Optimization (PGO)**: Runtime-guided code optimization

**Key Principles:**
- Always measure before optimizing
- Focus on hot paths identified through profiling
- Consider trade-offs between memory and CPU
- Use appropriate data structures for the workload

examples.description: Comparing string concatenation methods with memory allocation analysis
Thread-safe object pool implementation for expensive-to-create objects
Efficient integer parsing without string allocations

interview.q: How do you profile a .NET application in production?
interview.a: Use dotnet-trace for CPU profiling and event collection, dotnet-counters for real-time metrics monitoring, and dotnet-dump for memory analysis. Configure Application Insights for distributed tracing and use Performance Counters for system-level monitoring.

interview.q: When should you use object pooling versus letting GC handle objects?
interview.a: Use object pooling when: 1) Objects are expensive to create/destroy, 2) You have frequent allocations in hot paths, 3) GC pressure is causing Gen2 collections, 4) Objects are large (>85KB for LOH). Let GC handle small, short-lived objects where pooling overhead outweighs benefits.

interview.q: What's the difference between Span<T> and Memory<T> and when to use each?
interview.a: Span<T> is stack-only (ref struct) for synchronous, hot-path operations. Memory<T> can be stored on heap for async operations. Use Span for performance-critical synchronous code, Memory when you need to store the buffer across async operations or in heap-allocated objects.

interview.q: How do you optimize async/await performance?
interview.a: 1) Use ValueTask for hot paths with synchronous completion, 2) ConfigureAwait(false) for library code, 3) Avoid async void methods, 4) Use cancellation tokens appropriately, 5) Consider Task.WhenAll for parallel operations, 6) Profile with async state machine allocations in mind.

interview.q: What are the performance implications of different collection types?
interview.a: List<T>: Good for indexed access, poor for inserts. LinkedList<T>: Good for frequent inserts/deletes, poor for random access. Dictionary<T>: O(1) lookups but hashing overhead. HashSet<T>: Fast uniqueness checks. Sorted collections: O(log n) operations. Choose based on access patterns.

interview.q: How do you diagnose and fix memory leaks in .NET?
interview.a: 1) Use dotnet-dump to capture memory snapshots, 2) Analyze with Visual Studio Diagnostic Tools or PerfView, 3) Check for event handler subscriptions, 4) Review static collections, 5) Look at finalizer queues, 6) Use WeakReference for caches, 7) Implement IDisposable pattern correctly.

interview.q: What is tiered compilation and how does it affect performance?
interview.a: Tiered compilation starts with quick JIT (Tier 0) for faster startup, then recompiles hot methods with optimizations (Tier 1). Enable for better steady-state performance but monitor startup time. Configure with MSBuild properties or runtime settings.

interview.q: How do you optimize database access performance in .NET?
interview.a: 1) Use connection pooling, 2) Implement proper indexing, 3) Use Dapper or EF Core with AsNoTracking for reads, 4) Batch operations where possible, 5) Use IAsyncEnumerable for streaming large results, 6) Implement caching strategies, 7) Monitor query performance with Application Insights.

interview.q: What are the performance benefits of using structs vs classes?
interview.a: Structs: Stack allocation (no GC), better cache locality, no heap fragmentation. Classes: Reference semantics, inheritance, nullability. Use structs for small, immutable data in hot paths. Beware of boxing and copying overhead with large structs.

interview.q: How do you use SIMD instructions in .NET for performance optimization?
interview.a: Use System.Numerics.Vector<T> for hardware-accelerated operations on arrays. Works with Vector.Create, Vector.Add, Vector.Multiply etc. Check Vector.IsHardwareAccelerated. Best for numerical computations, image processing, and scientific calculations.

interview.q: What are the best practices for minimizing GC pressure?
interview.a: 1) Use stackalloc for small buffers, 2) Implement object pooling, 3) Reuse collections with Clear(), 4) Avoid closures capturing heap variables, 5) Use struct enumerators, 6) Prefer string.Create over concatenation, 7) Use ArrayPool for temporary arrays.

interview.q: How do you implement efficient caching in .NET?
interview.a: 1) Use MemoryCache for in-memory caching, 2) Implement IDistributedCache for distributed scenarios, 3) Set appropriate expiration policies, 4) Use cache stampede prevention, 5) Consider LazyCache or FusionCache libraries, 6) Monitor cache hit ratios.

interview.q: What tools do you use for load testing .NET applications?
interview.a: 1) k6 for scriptable load testing, 2) JMeter for complex scenarios, 3) Visual Studio Load Testing (deprecated), 4) NBomber for .NET-specific testing, 5) Apache Bench for quick tests, 6) Integrated with CI/CD pipelines for performance regression detection.

interview.q: How do you optimize JSON serialization/deserialization?
interview.a: 1) Use source generators (System.Text.Json.SourceGeneration), 2) Prefer Utf8JsonReader/Writer for streaming, 3) Reuse JsonSerializerOptions, 4) Use pooling for buffers, 5) Consider ProtoBuf or MessagePack for binary serialization, 6) Benchmark different serializers for your data.

interview.q: What are the performance implications of reflection and how to optimize?
interview.a: Reflection is slow (10-100x slower). Optimize by: 1) Caching reflection results, 2) Using Delegate.CreateDelegate, 3) Generating expression trees, 4) Using source generators, 5) Implementing interfaces instead, 6) Using TypeDescriptor or compiled lambdas.

interview.q: How do you handle high-concurrency scenarios in .NET?
interview.a: 1) Use async/await for I/O-bound work, 2) Implement backpressure with SemaphoreSlim, 3) Use Channel<T> for producer-consumer patterns, 4) Consider System.Threading.Channels, 5) Use Concurrent collections, 6) Implement rate limiting, 7) Monitor thread pool saturation.

interview.q: What is PGO (Profile-Guided Optimization) and how to use it?
interview.a: PGO uses runtime profiling data to optimize code. Enable with <TieredPGO>true</TieredPGO> and <DynamicPGO>true</DynamicPGO>. Generates optimized native code based on actual usage patterns. Requires training runs with representative workloads.

interview.q: How do you optimize startup time for .NET applications?
interview.a: 1) Use ReadyToRun compilation, 2) Trim unused assemblies, 3) Lazy load modules, 4) Minimize static constructors, 5) Use Native AOT for smallest footprint, 6) Optimize DI container registration, 7) Profile startup with PerfView or dotnet-trace.

interview.q: What are the performance considerations when using Dependency Injection?
interview.a: 1) Use Transient for lightweight, stateless services, 2) Scoped for request-bound resources, 3) Singleton for shared heavy resources, 4) Avoid service location pattern, 5) Use compiled lambdas for factory methods, 6) Consider source-generated DI containers for AOT.

interview.q: How do you implement real-time performance monitoring?
interview.a: 1) Expose metrics endpoints with /metrics, 2) Use System.Diagnostics.Metrics API, 3) Integrate with Prometheus/Grafana, 4) Implement health checks with liveness/readiness probes, 5) Use Application Insights Live Metrics, 6) Set up alerting on key performance indicators.

csharp_deployment.title: Deployment & Containers
csharp_deployment.summary: Comprehensive deployment strategies for .NET applications including self-contained deployments, containerization with Docker, multi-stage builds, orchestration with Kubernetes, and CI/CD pipeline implementation. Covers cloud deployment options, configuration management, and production best practices.
csharp_deployment.details:
**Deployment Models:**
1. **Framework-Dependent Deployments**
   - Requires .NET runtime installed on host
   - Smaller deployment size
   - Shared runtime updates
   - Use: dotnet publish --self-contained false

2. **Self-Contained Deployments**
   - Includes .NET runtime in deployment
   - Larger size but portable
   - Independent runtime version
   - Use: dotnet publish --self-contained true -r <RID>

3. **Single-File Applications**
   - All dependencies in single executable
   - Simplified distribution
   - Extraction to temp directory on first run
   - Use: dotnet publish -p:PublishSingleFile=true

**Containerization:**
1. **Docker Best Practices**
   - Multi-stage builds for minimal images
   - Use official .NET images from MCR
   - Non-root user execution
   - Health check implementation
   - Proper layer caching

2. **Base Image Selection**
   - **mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet**: Runtime only (smallest)
   - **mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/runtime**: Console app runtime
   - **mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk**: Build-time only
   - **Distroless variants**: Minimal attack surface

3. **Orchestration with Kubernetes**
   - Deployment manifests and Helm charts
   - Resource limits and requests
   - Liveness/readiness probes
   - Horizontal Pod Autoscaling
   - Service mesh integration (Istio, Linkerd)

**CI/CD Pipeline:**
1. **Build Pipeline**
   - Restore dependencies with caching
   - Run tests and code coverage
   - Security scanning (SAST, SCA)
   - Build artifacts with versioning

2. **Release Pipeline**
   - Environment-specific configurations
   - Blue-green or canary deployments
   - Database migration strategies
   - Rollback procedures

**Configuration Management:**
1. **Environment Variables**
   - 12-factor app principles
   - Kubernetes ConfigMaps and Secrets
   - Azure Key Vault / AWS Secrets Manager

2. **Configuration Providers**
   - appsettings.json with environment overrides
   - User Secrets for development
   - Command-line arguments
   - Hierarchical configuration binding

**Monitoring & Observability:**
1. **Logging Strategy**
   - Structured logging with Serilog/OpenTelemetry
   - Correlation IDs for distributed tracing
   - Log aggregation (ELK stack, Seq)

2. **Metrics Collection**
   - Prometheus metrics endpoint
   - Application Insights integration
   - Custom business metrics

**Security Considerations:**
1. **Image Security**
   - Scan for vulnerabilities (Trivy, Grype)
   - Sign images with Cosign
   - Use trusted base images

2. **Runtime Security**
   - Least privilege principle
   - Network policies in Kubernetes
   - Secrets management
   - Regular security updates
csharp_deployment.cheat:
// Common Deployment Commands:
// Framework-dependent: dotnet publish -c Release --self-contained false
// Self-contained: dotnet publish -c Release --self-contained true -r linux-x64
// Single file: dotnet publish -c Release -p:PublishSingleFile=true --self-contained true
// Trimmed: dotnet publish -c Release -p:PublishTrimmed=true
// ReadyToRun: dotnet publish -c Release -p:PublishReadyToRun=true

// Docker Multi-Stage Build:
# Build stage
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0 AS build
WORKDIR /src
COPY *.csproj .
RUN dotnet restore
COPY . .
RUN dotnet publish -c Release -o /app

# Runtime stage
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /app .
USER 1000
EXPOSE 8080
HEALTHCHECK CMD curl --fail http://localhost:8080/health || exit 1
ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "MyApp.dll"]

// Kubernetes Deployment:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: myapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: myapp
        image: myregistry/myapp:1.0.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "100m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/live
            port: 8080
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/ready
            port: 8080
